docs.title: Dift Documentation
docs.text:
Dift is an open-source CLI platform for dataset comparison, drift detection, and data trust validation.

It helps data teams quickly understand:

  • what changed
  • why it matters
  • whether new data is safe to trust

Dift supports:

  • local files
  • SQL databases
  • DuckDB
  • BigQuery
  • warehouse workflows
  • automation pipelines
  • batch dataset validation
  • reusable comparison profiles
  • scheduled comparisons
  • drift monitoring workflows

docs.title: Dataset Comparison
docs.text:
Compare datasets across:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel
  • JSON
  • SQL databases
  • DuckDB
  • BigQuery
  • cloud warehouse workflows

docs.title: Drift Detection
docs.text:
Dift detects:

  • numeric drift
  • categorical drift
  • frequency shifts
  • outlier spikes
  • schema changes
  • row-level changes
  • null spikes
  • duplicate spikes

docs.title: Risk Analysis
docs.text:
Dift converts dataset changes into understandable risk levels:

  • low
  • medium
  • high

This helps teams prioritize risky dataset changes before they impact production systems.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
Generate reports in multiple formats:

  • Console
  • JSON
  • CSV
  • Excel
  • HTML

HTML reports support multiple templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Automation Workflows
docs.text:
Dift supports:

  • CI/CD integration
  • scheduled comparisons
  • batch comparisons
  • reusable profiles
  • reusable configs
  • comparison history tracking
  • strict exit codes
  • non-interactive execution

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv --key customer_id

Generate an HTML report:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --output report.html

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift is designed to help teams build trust in data.

The goal is not only to detect changes - but to explain their operational risk clearly and consistently.

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
Dift is fully open source and community-driven.

Contributions are welcome.

GitHub:

https://github.com/ReginaldErzoah/Dift

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
Dift is designed to support automation-first data validation workflows.

This document explains how to integrate Dift into:

  • CI/CD pipelines
  • cron jobs
  • scheduled workflows
  • Airflow
  • Jenkins
  • GitHub Actions
  • Prefect
  • Dagster
  • enterprise monitoring systems

docs.title: Why Automation Matters
docs.text:
Modern data systems continuously evolve.

Bad data can silently break:

  • dashboards
  • ML models
  • ETL pipelines
  • reports
  • warehouse transformations
  • production analytics

Dift helps automate trust validation before bad data propagates downstream.

docs.title: Core Automation Features
docs.text:
Dift automation features include:

  • reusable profiles
  • scheduled comparisons
  • strict exit codes
  • quiet mode
  • no-color mode
  • comparison history
  • batch workflows
  • reusable configs
  • environment-aware execution

docs.title: Automation Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift automation workflows prioritize:

  • reproducibility
  • machine-readable outputs
  • predictable execution
  • CI/CD compatibility
  • non-interactive workflows

docs.title: Non-Interactive CLI Execution
docs.text:
Dift fully supports non-interactive execution.

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --strict-exit-codes \
  --quiet \
  --no-color

This is ideal for:

  • cron jobs
  • CI/CD systems
  • scheduled monitoring
  • automated warehouse checks

docs.title: Strict Exit Codes
docs.text:
Strict exit codes allow Dift to fail workflows automatically when risky drift is detected.

Enable strict mode:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --strict-exit-codes

docs.title: Exit Code Mapping
docs.text: Exit Code Meaning 0 Low-risk comparison 1 Medium-risk drift detected 2 High-risk drift detected 3 Runtime error or failed comparison

docs.title: Why Strict Exit Codes Matter
docs.text:
Strict exit codes allow systems to automatically:

  • fail CI jobs
  • stop deployments
  • block ETL workflows
  • trigger alerts
  • enforce validation gates

docs.title: Quiet Mode
docs.text:
Suppress non-error output:

dift old.csv new.csv --quiet

Useful for:

  • automation logs
  • scheduled workflows
  • CI environments

docs.title: No-Color Mode
docs.text:
Disable ANSI terminal colors:

dift old.csv new.csv --no-color

Useful for:

  • plain-text logging systems
  • CI logs
  • centralized observability platforms

docs.title: Recommended Automation Command
docs.text:
Recommended production automation workflow:

dift prod.csv staging.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --strict-exit-codes \
  --quiet \
  --no-color

docs.title: Reusable Profiles
docs.text:
Profiles simplify recurring workflows.

Create profile:

dift profile create nightly-check \
  --old prod.csv \
  --new candidate.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html

Run profile:

dift profile run nightly-check

docs.title: Why Profiles Matter
docs.text:
Profiles help standardize:

  • nightly validations
  • production checks
  • warehouse monitoring
  • recurring comparisons

docs.title: Scheduled Comparisons
docs.text:
Dift supports reusable schedule generation workflows.

Generate cron command:

dift schedule cron nightly-check

Example output:

0 2 * * * dift profile run nightly-check --history --strict-exit-codes

docs.title: Custom Schedule Times
docs.text:
Generate custom schedules:

dift schedule cron nightly-check \
  --hour 5 \
  --minute 30

docs.title: Saved Schedules
docs.text:
Create reusable schedules:

dift schedule create daily-check \
  --profile nightly-check \
  --cron "0 2 * * *"

docs.title: List Schedules
docs.text: dift schedule list

docs.title: Run Saved Schedule
docs.text: dift schedule run daily-check

docs.title: Delete Schedule
docs.text: dift schedule delete daily-check

docs.title: Cron Integration
docs.text:
Open cron editor:

crontab -e

Add generated schedule:

0 2 * * * dift profile run nightly-check --history --strict-exit-codes

docs.title: Linux/macOS Workflow Example
docs.text:
Example nightly validation:

0 2 * * * dift profile run production-check \
  --history \
  --strict-exit-codes \
  --quiet \
  --no-color

docs.title: Windows Task Scheduler
docs.text:
Use generated Dift commands directly inside:

  • Windows Task Scheduler
  • PowerShell automation
  • scheduled batch workflows

Example:

dift profile run nightly-check --history --strict-exit-codes

docs.title: GitHub Actions Integration
docs.text:
Example workflow:

name: Dift Validation

on:
  push:

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Dift
        run: pip install dift-cli

      - name: Run Validation
        run: |
          dift old.csv new.csv \
            --key customer_id \
            --strict-exit-codes \
            --quiet \
            --no-color

docs.title: Jenkins Integration
docs.text:
Example Jenkins pipeline step:

stage('Validate Data') {
    steps {
        sh '''
        dift prod.csv staging.csv \
          --key customer_id \
          --strict-exit-codes
        '''
    }
}

docs.title: Airflow Integration
docs.text:
Dift integrates naturally into Airflow workflows.

Example:

from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator

validate_data = BashOperator(
    task_id="validate_data",
    bash_command="""
    dift old.csv new.csv \
      --key customer_id \
      --strict-exit-codes
    """
)

docs.title: Airflow Use Cases
docs.text:
Common Airflow workflows:

  • ETL validation
  • warehouse regression testing
  • production dataset checks
  • scheduled monitoring

docs.title: Prefect Integration
docs.text:
Example:

from prefect import flow
import subprocess

@flow
def validate():
    subprocess.run(
        [
            "dift",
            "old.csv",
            "new.csv",
            "--strict-exit-codes"
        ],
        check=True,
    )

docs.title: Dagster Integration
docs.text:
Example:

import subprocess

subprocess.run(
    [
        "dift",
        "old.csv",
        "new.csv",
        "--strict-exit-codes",
    ],
    check=True,
)

docs.title: Batch Comparison Automation
docs.text:
Batch workflows are ideal for:

  • warehouse snapshots
  • multi-table ETL validation
  • large monitoring workflows

Example:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --strict-exit-codes

docs.title: Batch HTML Reports
docs.text:
Example:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/batch

docs.title: Comparison History
docs.text:
Persist historical validation runs:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --history

docs.title: Why History Tracking Matters
docs.text:
History tracking supports:

  • trend analysis
  • recurring risk visibility
  • long-term monitoring
  • compliance workflows

docs.title: History Directory
docs.text:
Default location:

.dift/history/history.jsonl

docs.title: Custom History Location
docs.text:
Example:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --history \
  --history-dir reports/history

docs.title: Environment-Aware Automation
docs.text:
Dift supports environment-specific configs.

Example:

environments:
  development:
    threshold: 0.2

  production:
    threshold: 0.05

Run:

dift --config config.yaml --env production

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Dift supports environment variable interpolation.

Example:

old_dataset: ${OLD_DATASET}
new_dataset: ${NEW_DATASET}

docs.title: CI/CD-Friendly Configs
docs.text:
Configs help centralize automation behavior.

Example:

threshold: 0.1

Run:

dift --config ci_config.yaml

docs.text:

docs.text:

docs.title: Batch Comparisons
docs.text:
Dift supports batch dataset comparison workflows for validating multiple dataset pairs in a single command.

Batch workflows help teams:

  • validate ETL pipelines
  • monitor warehouse snapshots
  • compare multiple tables automatically
  • run scheduled quality checks
  • generate large-scale reports
  • automate regression testing

docs.title: What Is Batch Comparison?
docs.text:
Batch comparison allows Dift to:

  • scan two directories
  • match datasets automatically
  • run comparisons in sequence
  • generate reports for each dataset pair

Dift matches files by filename.

Example:

old/customers.csv  <-->  new/customers.csv

docs.title: Example Folder Structure
docs.text:
data/
├── old/
│   ├── customers.csv
│   ├── orders.csv
│   └── products.csv
│
└── new/
    ├── customers.csv
    ├── orders.csv
    └── products.csv

docs.title: Run Batch Comparison
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --key customer_id

Dift automatically:

  • matches files
  • compares datasets
  • calculates risk
  • detects drift
  • generates output

docs.title: Generate HTML Reports
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/

docs.title: Example Output Structure
docs.text:
reports/
├── customers/
│   └── dift_report.html
├── orders/
│   └── dift_report.html
└── products/
    └── dift_report.html

docs.title: Generate JSON Reports
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report json \
  --output-dir reports/json

docs.title: Generate CSV Reports
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report csv \
  --output-dir reports/csv

docs.title: Generate Excel Reports
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report excel \
  --output-dir reports/excel

docs.title: HTML Templates
docs.text:
Customize batch HTML reports:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report html \
  --template enterprise \
  --output-dir reports/

Available templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Continue On Error
docs.text: By default, Dift continues running comparisons even if one dataset fails.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --continue-on-error

Useful for:

  • large pipelines
  • scheduled monitoring
  • partial dataset failures

docs.title: Stop On Error
docs.text:
Stop immediately after the first failure:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --stop-on-error

Useful for:

  • strict validation workflows
  • CI/CD pipelines
  • production gates

docs.title: Batch Comparison History
docs.text: Save historical comparison records during batch execution.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --history \
  --history-dir reports/history

docs.title: Example History Structure
docs.text:
reports/
└── history/
    ├── customers/
    │   └── history.jsonl
    ├── orders/
    │   └── history.jsonl
    └── products/
        └── history.jsonl

docs.title: Batch + Strict Exit Codes
docs.text: Enable automation-friendly exit codes.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --strict-exit-codes

Exit codes:

Code Meaning 0 Low risk 1 Medium risk 2 High risk 3 Runtime error

docs.title: Quiet Mode
docs.text:
Suppress non-error output:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --quiet

Useful for:

  • CI/CD systems
  • cron jobs
  • automated monitoring

docs.title: Disable Colored Output
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --no-color

Useful for:

  • plain-text logs
  • CI systems
  • terminal compatibility

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Dift provides lightweight progress indicators during batch workflows.

Examples:

  • dataset loading
  • warehouse extraction
  • schema comparison
  • drift analysis
  • report generation

Useful for:

  • large datasets
  • warehouse monitoring
  • long-running workflows

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
Batch workflows support:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel
  • JSON

docs.title: Supported Connectors
docs.text:
Batch workflows can integrate with:

  • DuckDB
  • SQL databases
  • BigQuery
  • warehouse workflows

depending on your dataset structure.

docs.title: Example ETL Validation Workflow
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir warehouse_snapshot_1 \
  --new-dir warehouse_snapshot_2 \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/

Useful for:

  • ETL regression testing
  • warehouse validation
  • production monitoring

docs.title: Example ML Validation Workflow
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir train_v1 \
  --new-dir train_v2 \
  --threshold 0.03

Useful for:

  • feature drift detection
  • training validation
  • ML dataset governance

docs.title: Example CI/CD Workflow
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir prod \
  --new-dir candidate \
  --report json \
  --strict-exit-codes \
  --quiet

docs.title: File Matching Rules
docs.text:
Dift matches datasets using filenames.

Example:

old/customers.csv
new/customers.csv

Files without matches may generate warnings.

docs.title: Validation Errors
docs.text:
Dift provides actionable validation messages.

Examples:

Error: Batch directory not found

Error: No matching datasets found

Error: Unsupported dataset type '.txt'

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Recommended practices:

  • standardize filenames
  • separate environments clearly
  • enable history tracking
  • use strict exit codes in CI/CD
  • generate reports into isolated directories
  • use templates for stakeholder reporting

docs.title: ETL Validation
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir before \
  --new-dir after

docs.title: Warehouse Monitoring
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir snapshots/day1 \
  --new-dir snapshots/day2

docs.title: Scheduled Quality Checks
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir prod \
  --new-dir nightly

docs.title: Historical Drift Monitoring
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --history

docs.title: Example Enterprise Workflow
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir warehouse/prod \
  --new-dir warehouse/staging \
  --report html \
  --template enterprise \
  --history \
  --strict-exit-codes \
  --output-dir reports/

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Dift supports reusable configuration files for cleaner, reproducible, and automation-friendly workflows.

Configuration files help teams:

  • reduce repetitive CLI commands
  • standardize validation rules
  • simplify CI/CD workflows
  • manage environments
  • reuse comparison settings
  • centralize drift policies

docs.title: Supported Configuration Formats
docs.text:
Dift supports:

  • YAML (.yaml, .yml)
  • TOML (.toml)
  • JSON (.json)

docs.title: YAML
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv

key: customer_id

threshold: 0.1

report: html
output: reports/report.html

Run:

dift --config config.yaml

docs.title: TOML Example
docs.text:
old_dataset = "examples/old.csv"
new_dataset = "examples/new.csv"

key = "customer_id"

threshold = 0.1

report = "json"
output = "reports/report.json"

docs.title: JSON Example
docs.text:
{
  "old_dataset": "examples/old.csv",
  "new_dataset": "examples/new.csv",
  "key": "customer_id",
  "threshold": 0.1,
  "report": "csv",
  "output": "reports/report.csv"
}

docs.title: Dataset Paths in Config Files
docs.text:
Dift can load dataset paths directly from configuration files.

This allows reusable workflows without typing datasets repeatedly.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv

key: customer_id
report: html

Run:

dift --config config.yaml

docs.title: CLI Override Behavior
docs.text: CLI arguments override configuration values.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift prod.csv staging.csv \
  --config config.yaml \
  --report json \
  --output override.json

Dift will use:

  • datasets from CLI
  • report/output from CLI
  • remaining values from config

docs.title: Configuration Priority
docs.text:
Dift resolves settings using:

CLI Arguments > Profiles > Config Files > Defaults

This provides flexibility while keeping workflows reproducible.

docs.title: Supported Configuration Fields
docs.text: Field Description old_dataset Original dataset new_dataset New dataset key Row matching key threshold Numeric drift threshold report Report format output Report output path output_dir Output directory template HTML template history Enable history tracking history_dir History directory strict_exit_codes Risk-based exit codes quiet Suppress non-error output no_color Disable terminal colors

docs.title: Threshold Configurations
docs.text: Dift supports reusable threshold policies.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

docs.title: Column-Level Threshold Overrides
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1

  columns:
    revenue:
      numeric: 0.05

    transactions:
      outlier: 0.03

Useful for:

  • sensitive metrics
  • production monitoring
  • anomaly-heavy datasets

docs.title: Environment-Based Configurations
docs.text:
Dift supports environment-specific workflows.

Useful for:

  • development
  • staging
  • production
  • CI/CD pipelines

docs.title: Example Environment Config
docs.text:
key: customer_id
report: html

environments:
  development:
    old_dataset: examples/dev_old.csv
    new_dataset: examples/dev_new.csv
    threshold: 0.2

  staging:
    old_dataset: staging_old.csv
    new_dataset: staging_new.csv
    threshold: 0.15

  production:
    old_dataset: prod_old.csv
    new_dataset: prod_new.csv
    threshold: 0.1

docs.title: Select Environment
docs.text: dift --config config_env.yaml --env production

docs.title: Environment Variable Support
docs.text: Dift supports environment variable interpolation.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
old_dataset: ${OLD_DATASET}
new_dataset: ${NEW_DATASET}

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text:
export OLD_DATASET=data/old.csv
export NEW_DATASET=data/new.csv

docs.title: Windows PowerShell
docs.text:
$env:OLD_DATASET="data/old.csv"
$env:NEW_DATASET="data/new.csv"

docs.title: Run
docs.text: dift --config config_env.yaml

docs.title: Missing Environment Variables
docs.text:
If a required variable is missing, Dift shows a helpful validation error.

Example:

Error: Missing environment variable 'OLD_DATASET'

docs.title: Output Directory Support
docs.text:
Save reports into directories:

report: html
output_dir: reports/

Generated filename:

reports/dift_report.html

docs.title: HTML Templates
docs.text: Specify HTML templates directly inside configs.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
report: html
template: enterprise

Available templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: History Tracking
docs.text: Enable persistent comparison history.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
history: true
history_dir: reports/history

docs.title: Strict Exit Codes
docs.text:
Automation-friendly validation:

strict_exit_codes: true

Exit codes:

Code Meaning 0 Low risk 1 Medium risk 2 High risk 3 Runtime error

docs.title: Quiet Mode
docs.text:
Useful for automation workflows.

quiet: true

docs.title: Disable Colors
docs.text:
no_color: true

Useful for:

  • CI logs
  • plain-text terminals
  • log aggregation systems

docs.title: Example Full Configuration
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv

key: customer_id

report: html
template: enterprise
output: reports/report.html

thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

history: true
history_dir: reports/history

strict_exit_codes: true
quiet: false
no_color: false

docs.title: Comparison History
docs.text:
Dift supports persistent comparison history tracking for monitoring dataset drift and risk over time.

History tracking helps teams:

  • monitor recurring drift
  • analyze long-term risk trends
  • audit validation workflows
  • investigate incidents
  • track dataset trust evolution
  • build governance workflows

docs.title: Why History Tracking Matters
docs.text:
Modern datasets change constantly.

Without historical tracking, teams often cannot answer:

  • When did drift begin?
  • Has this issue happened before?
  • Is risk increasing over time?
  • Which datasets are unstable?
  • Which pipelines regress frequently?

Dift history tracking helps answer these questions.

docs.title: Enable History Tracking
docs.text:
Save comparison history automatically:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --history

docs.title: Default History Location
docs.text:
By default, Dift stores history in:

.dift/history/history.jsonl

docs.title: Custom History Directory
docs.text:
Specify a custom history location:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --history \
  --history-dir reports/history

Useful for:

  • CI/CD systems
  • centralized storage
  • audit workflows
  • scheduled monitoring

docs.title: View History Records
docs.text:
List saved comparison runs:

dift history list

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
1. 2026-05-15T12:30:00Z | risk=medium | old.csv -> new.csv
2. 2026-05-16T08:10:00Z | risk=high | prod.csv -> staging.csv
3. 2026-05-17T03:45:00Z | risk=low | train_v1.csv -> train_v2.csv

docs.title: Show Detailed History Entry
docs.text:
Inspect a specific comparison record:

dift history show 1

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
{
  "timestamp": "2026-05-15T12:30:00Z",
  "risk_level": "medium",
  "old_dataset": "old.csv",
  "new_dataset": "new.csv",
  "row_delta": 150,
  "schema_changes": 2,
  "warnings": [
    "Numeric drift detected in revenue"
  ]
}

docs.title: Clear History
docs.text:
Delete all saved history:

dift history clear

Useful for:

  • resetting environments
  • test workflows
  • temporary monitoring setups

docs.title: Batch Comparison History
docs.text: History tracking also works with batch comparisons.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --history

docs.title: Batch History Structure
docs.text:
reports/
└── history/
    ├── customers/
    │   └── history.jsonl
    ├── orders/
    │   └── history.jsonl
    └── products/
        └── history.jsonl

docs.title: What Gets Stored
docs.text:
History records may include:

  • timestamp
  • dataset paths
  • comparison runtime
  • risk level
  • row statistics
  • schema changes
  • drift warnings
  • threshold metadata
  • report configuration

docs.title: Historical Risk Monitoring
docs.text:
Track risk changes over time.

Example:

Day 1 → LOW
Day 2 → MEDIUM
Day 3 → HIGH

Useful for:

  • anomaly detection
  • pipeline instability monitoring
  • warehouse validation
  • ML data governance

docs.title: Historical Drift Monitoring
docs.text:
Monitor recurring numeric and categorical drift.

Examples:

  • revenue drift
  • customer segmentation changes
  • feature distribution shifts
  • outlier spikes

docs.title: Historical Quality Monitoring
docs.text:
Track:

  • null spikes
  • duplicate spikes
  • schema instability
  • dataset volatility

Useful for:

  • data governance
  • SLA monitoring
  • reliability analysis

  --history \
  --strict-exit-codes

  • nightly validations

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide explains how to install Dift across different operating systems and environments.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Dift requires:

  • Python 3.10+
  • pip
  • terminal or shell access

Verify your Python version:

python --version

or

python3 --version

docs.title: Standard Installation
docs.text: pip install dift-cli

docs.title: Upgrade Dift
docs.text:
Upgrade to the latest version:

pip install --upgrade dift-cli

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Run:

dift --help

or:

python -m dift.cli --help

You should see the Dift CLI help output.

docs.title: Virtual Environment Setup
docs.text: Using a virtual environment is strongly recommended.

docs.title: Windows (Git Bash)
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/Scripts/activate
pip install dift-cli

docs.title: Windows (PowerShell)
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
pip install dift-cli

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text:
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install dift-cli

docs.title: pipx Installation (Recommended)
docs.text:
For isolated CLI environments:

pipx install dift-cli

Upgrade later:

pipx upgrade dift-cli

docs.title: Install From Source
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/ReginaldErzoah/Dift.git

Enter the project directory:

cd Dift

Create a virtual environment:

python -m venv .venv

Activate it.

docs.title: Windows (Git Bash)
docs.text: source .venv/Scripts/activate

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text:
source .venv/bin/activate

Install dependencies:

pip install -r requirements.txt

Install Dift in editable mode:

pip install -e .

docs.title: Optional Connector Dependencies
docs.text: Some connectors require additional packages.

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
pip install psycopg2-binary

or:

pip install psycopg

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text: pip install pymysql

docs.title: Redshift
docs.text: pip install sqlalchemy-redshift redshift-connector

docs.title: Snowflake
docs.text: pip install snowflake-sqlalchemy

docs.title: BigQuery
docs.text: pip install google-cloud-bigquery db-dtypes

docs.title: DuckDB
docs.text: pip install duckdb

docs.title: Install SQL Support
docs.text:
Core SQL support requires SQLAlchemy:

pip install sqlalchemy

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
Install development tools:

pip install -r requirements-dev.txt

Typical development tooling includes:

  • pytest
  • ruff
  • mypy

docs.title: Verify Development Environment
docs.text:
Run tests:

pytest

Run linting:

ruff check .

Run type checking:

mypy dift

docs.title: Python Version Too Old
docs.text:
Error example:

Python 3.9 is not supported

Solution:

Install Python 3.10 or newer.

docs.title: Command Not Found
docs.text:
Error example:

dift: command not found

Possible fixes:

docs.title: Reinstall
docs.text: pip install dift-cli

docs.title: Use Python Module Execution
docs.text: python -m dift.cli --help

docs.title: Verify PATH
docs.text: Ensure your Python scripts directory is available in your system PATH.

docs.title: Missing Connector Drivers
docs.text:
Error example:

PostgreSQL support requires psycopg2

Install the required driver:

pip install psycopg2-binary

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After installation:

  • Continue to the Quick Start guide
  • Learn dataset comparison workflows
  • Explore reports and automation features
  • Configure connectors and warehouses

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Basic comparison:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv --key customer_id

Generate an HTML report:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --output report.html

docs.title: Profiles
docs.text:
Dift supports reusable comparison profiles for automating recurring dataset validation workflows.

Profiles help teams:

  • avoid repetitive CLI commands
  • standardize comparisons
  • automate recurring checks
  • simplify scheduled workflows
  • reuse report configurations
  • build production validation pipelines

docs.title: What Are Profiles?
docs.text:
A profile stores comparison settings such as:

  • datasets
  • keys
  • thresholds
  • report formats
  • output locations
  • automation options

Instead of repeatedly typing long CLI commands, you can reuse a saved profile.

docs.title: Create a Profile
docs.text: Use profile create to save a reusable workflow.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift profile create nightly-check \
  --old examples/old.csv \
  --new examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --threshold 0.1

This creates a reusable profile named:

nightly-check

docs.title: Run a Profile
docs.text:
Execute a saved profile:

dift profile run nightly-check

Dift automatically loads all saved settings.

docs.title: List Profiles
docs.text:
View all saved profiles:

dift profile list

Example output:

- nightly-check
- production-validation
- warehouse-monitor

docs.title: Show Profile Details
docs.text:
Inspect a saved profile:

dift profile show nightly-check

Example output:

{
  "old_dataset": "examples/old.csv",

Field Description old_dataset Original dataset new_dataset New dataset key Row matching key threshold Numeric drift threshold report Report format output Report output path output_dir Output directory template HTML template history Enable history tracking history_dir History directory strict_exit_codes Automation-friendly exit codes quiet Suppress non-error output no_color Disable terminal colors

  • warehouse monitoring

  • scheduled audits

docs.title: Configuration Priority
docs.text:
Profiles participate in Dift’s configuration hierarchy.

Priority order:

CLI Arguments > Profiles > Config Files > Defaults

This means:

  • CLI options override profiles
  • profiles override config files
  • configs override defaults

docs.title: CLI Override Example
docs.text:
dift profile run nightly-check --report json

This temporarily overrides the saved report format.

docs.title: Profiles and Thresholds
docs.text:
Profiles can store:

  • global thresholds
  • categorical thresholds
  • outlier thresholds
  • reusable validation policies

dift profile create strict-monitor \
  --old prod.csv \
  --new candidate.csv \
  --threshold 0.03

Useful for:

  • financial data
  • ML feature monitoring
  • regulated environments

Profiles are stored locally by Dift.

This enables:

  • lightweight automation
  • local development workflows
  • environment-specific validation

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Profile execution supports progress indicators for:

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
This guide helps you get started with Dift quickly.

You will learn how to:

  • compare datasets
  • detect drift
  • generate reports
  • use configuration files
  • run batch comparisons
  • automate workflows

docs.title: Your First Comparison
docs.text:
Compare two CSV files:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv --key customer_id

This command compares:

  • schema changes
  • row changes
  • null spikes
  • duplicate spikes
  • drift patterns
  • outlier changes

docs.title: Understanding the --key
docs.text:
The --key option defines the column used to match rows across datasets.

Example:

--key customer_id

Typical keys:

  • customer_id
  • order_id
  • transaction_id
  • product_id

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
╭─────────────────────────╮
│ Dift Dataset Comparison │
│ Risk Level: MEDIUM      │
╰─────────────────────────╯

Warnings

Numeric drift:
'revenue'
mean shift 900.00%
(high, threshold 0.1)

Outlier spike:
'revenue' increased by 100.00%
(high)

Categorical shift:
'segment' max frequency shift 60.00%
(high)

docs.title: JSON Report
docs.text:
dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report json \
  --output report.json

docs.title: CSV Report
docs.text:
dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report csv \
  --output report.csv

docs.title: Excel Report

  --key customer_id \
  --report excel \
  --output report.xlsx

docs.title: HTML Report

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --output report.html

docs.title: HTML Templates
docs.text:
Customize HTML report appearance:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark \
  --output report.html

Available templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Drift Thresholds
docs.text:
Control drift sensitivity using --threshold.

Default threshold:

0.1

Example:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --threshold 0.2

Lower values detect smaller changes.

Higher values reduce sensitivity.

docs.title: Output Directory Support
docs.text:
Save reports into a directory with auto-generated filenames:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/

Generated filenames include:

  • dift_report.json
  • dift_report.csv
  • dift_report.xlsx
  • dift_report.html

docs.title: Using Configuration Files
docs.text: Run comparisons using reusable configuration files.

docs.title: YAML Example
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv
key: customer_id
threshold: 0.1
report: html

Run:

dift --config examples/config_sample.yaml

docs.title: Environment-Based Configs
docs.text:
Select reusable environments:

dift --config examples/config_env.yaml --env production

Useful for:

  • development
  • staging
  • production
  • CI/CD workflows

dift profile create nightly-check \

docs.title: Reports
docs.text:
Dift provides multiple report formats for dataset comparison, drift analysis, validation workflows, and automation pipelines.

Reports help teams:

  • understand dataset changes
  • identify drift risks
  • validate ETL workflows
  • monitor production datasets
  • share validation results
  • automate quality checks

docs.title: Supported Report Formats
docs.text:
Dift supports:

  • Console
  • JSON
  • CSV
  • Excel
  • HTML

docs.title: Console Reports
docs.text:
Console reports are the default output format.

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

Console reports display:

  • schema changes
  • row changes
  • drift warnings
  • outlier spikes
  • quality issues
  • overall risk level

docs.text:

│ Dift Dataset Comparison │

Warnings

mean shift 900.00%
(high, threshold 0.1)

Outlier spike:
'revenue' increased by 100.00%
(high)

Categorical shift:
'segment' max frequency shift 60.00%
(high)

  • APIs

  • machine-readable workflows

docs.text:

docs.text:
Reports include metadata such as:

docs.title: Risk Levels
docs.text:
Dift classifies comparisons into:

Risk Level Meaning low Safe dataset changes medium Moderate drift detected high Significant risk detected

docs.text:

  • numeric drift
  • categorical drift
  • outlier spikes
  • frequency shifts
  • null spikes

  • row additions/removals

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
This roadmap outlines the planned evolution of Dift from an open-source dataset comparison CLI into a comprehensive data trust and validation platform.

The roadmap reflects both completed milestones and future platform direction.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Dift aims to become the open-source standard for:

  • dataset regression testing
  • data drift monitoring
  • ML data validation
  • warehouse trust validation
  • automated data quality enforcement
  • data deployment validation
  • dataset observability

docs.title: Roadmap Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift development focuses on:

  • trust-first validation
  • automation-friendly workflows
  • scalable architecture
  • warehouse interoperability
  • extensibility
  • developer experience
  • enterprise readiness
  • open ecosystem growth

docs.title: Scheduled Comparisons
docs.text:
Dift supports reusable scheduled comparison workflows for automation, monitoring, CI/CD pipelines, and recurring data validation.

Scheduling helps teams:

  • run nightly drift checks
  • automate production validations
  • monitor warehouse stability
  • build recurring governance workflows
  • integrate with cron jobs
  • create CI/CD monitoring pipelines

docs.title: Why Scheduling Matters
docs.text:
Modern data systems change continuously.

Without scheduling, teams often:

  • detect drift too late
  • miss silent regressions
  • discover broken pipelines manually
  • lose visibility into data quality trends

Dift scheduling enables proactive monitoring.

docs.title: Scheduling Workflow Overview
docs.text:
Dift scheduling is built around reusable profiles.

Typical workflow:

  1. Create a profile
  2. Create a schedule
  3. Run automatically
  4. Track history
  5. Trigger alerts or pipeline failures

docs.title: Create a Comparison Profile
docs.text: First create a reusable comparison profile.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift profile create nightly-check \
  --old examples/old.csv \
  --new examples/new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report html \
  --output reports/nightly.html

This saves comparison settings for reuse.

docs.title: Generate a Cron Schedule
docs.text: Generate a cron-ready automation command.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: dift schedule cron nightly-check

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
0 2 * * * dift profile run nightly-check --history --strict-exit-codes

Meaning:

  • run daily
  • at 2:00 AM
  • save comparison history
  • fail pipelines on risky drift

docs.title: Custom Schedule Times
docs.text: Customize cron generation.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift schedule cron nightly-check \
  --hour 5 \
  --minute 30

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
30 5 * * * dift profile run nightly-check --history --strict-exit-codes

Runs daily at 5:30 AM.

docs.title: Create a Saved Schedule
docs.text: Store reusable schedules.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift schedule create daily-check \
  --profile nightly-check \
  --cron "0 2 * * *"

docs.title: List Saved Schedules
docs.text: dift schedule list

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
- daily-check
- warehouse-monitor
- ml-validation

docs.title: Show Schedule Details
docs.text: Inspect a saved schedule.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: dift schedule show daily-check

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
{
  "profile": "nightly-check",
  "cron": "0 2 * * *"
}

docs.title: Run a Schedule Manually
docs.text: Trigger a saved schedule immediately.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift schedule run daily-check

Useful for:

  • testing workflows
  • manual validation
  • debugging automation

docs.title: Delete a Schedule
docs.text: dift schedule delete daily-check

docs.title: Thresholds
docs.text:
Dift supports configurable drift thresholds for controlling comparison sensitivity.

Thresholds help teams:

  • detect subtle drift
  • reduce noisy alerts
  • standardize validation rules
  • customize risk policies
  • enforce production quality standards

docs.title: Why Thresholds Matter
docs.text:
Not every dataset change is equally important.

For example:

  • a 1% revenue shift may be critical
  • a 10% marketing category shift may be acceptable
  • outlier spikes may require aggressive detection

Thresholds help Dift determine:

  • when drift becomes risky
  • how severe changes are
  • whether alerts should be raised

docs.title: Default Threshold
docs.text:
Dift uses a default numeric drift threshold of:

0.1

This means:

  • drift above 10% is considered significant
  • lower values increase sensitivity
  • higher values reduce sensitivity

docs.title: CLI Threshold Usage
docs.text: Override the threshold directly from the CLI.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --threshold 0.2

This increases the drift threshold to 20%.

docs.title: Lower Threshold Example
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --threshold 0.03

Useful for:

  • ML features
  • financial metrics
  • sensitive production datasets

docs.title: Threshold Types
docs.text:
Dift supports multiple threshold categories.

Threshold Type Purpose numeric Numeric drift detection categorical Frequency distribution shifts outlier Outlier spike detection columns Column-specific overrides

docs.title: Threshold Configuration Files
docs.text: Thresholds can be stored in reusable configuration files.

docs.title: Global Threshold Example
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

docs.title: Column-Level Threshold Overrides
docs.text: Customize thresholds for specific columns.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

  columns:
    revenue:
      numeric: 0.03

    transactions:
      outlier: 0.05

    segment:
      categorical: 0.4

docs.title: Sensitive Revenue Monitoring
docs.text:
columns:
  revenue:
    numeric: 0.02

Detect even small revenue shifts.

docs.title: Strict Outlier Detection
docs.text:
columns:
  transactions:
    outlier: 0.03

Aggressively detect abnormal spikes.

docs.title: Relaxed Categorical Drift
docs.text:
columns:
  segment:
    categorical: 0.4

Reduce alerts for naturally variable categories.

docs.title: Full Threshold Configuration Example
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv

key: customer_id

report: html
output: reports/report.html

thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

  columns:
    revenue:
      numeric: 0.05
      outlier: 0.1

    segment:
      categorical: 0.3

Run:

dift --config config_thresholds.yaml

docs.title: CLI Override Behavior
docs.text: CLI thresholds override global numeric thresholds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dift --config config_thresholds.yaml --threshold 0.5

This overrides:

thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1

But preserves:

  • categorical thresholds
  • outlier thresholds
  • column-level overrides

docs.title: Numeric Drift Thresholds
docs.text:
Numeric thresholds affect:

  • mean shift detection
  • standard deviation drift
  • range shifts
  • statistical variation

docs.title: Categorical Thresholds
docs.text:
Categorical thresholds affect:

  • frequency shifts
  • category instability
  • new value detection
  • removed value detection

docs.title: Outlier Thresholds
docs.text:
Outlier thresholds affect:

  • IQR outlier spikes
  • anomaly growth
  • abnormal distributions
  • extreme value monitoring

docs.title: Threshold Severity
docs.text:
Dift uses thresholds to classify severity levels.

Severity Description low Minor drift medium Moderate drift high Significant drift

docs.title: Thresholds and Risk Scoring
docs.text:
Thresholds directly influence:

  • overall risk classification
  • warning generation
  • strict exit codes
  • automation workflows

docs.title: Conservative Detection
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.02

Detects small changes aggressively.

Useful for:

  • finance
  • ML features
  • regulated datasets

docs.title: Relaxed Detection
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.3

Reduces alert noise.

Useful for:

  • exploratory analytics
  • unstable datasets
  • low-risk environments

docs.title: Production Recommendations
docs.text:
Recommended production thresholds:

Workflow Suggested Threshold Financial data 0.01 - 0.05 ML features 0.03 - 0.1 ETL pipelines 0.05 - 0.15 Marketing analytics 0.15 - 0.3

docs.title: Team Standardization
docs.text:
Threshold configs help teams:

  • standardize policies
  • reuse validation rules
  • reduce alert inconsistency
  • simplify CI/CD validation
  • enforce governance

docs.title: Automation Workflows
docs.text:
Thresholds integrate with:

  • profiles
  • batch comparisons
  • scheduled comparisons
  • history tracking
  • strict exit codes

  --config production_thresholds.yaml \
  --strict-exit-codes

  numeric: 0.03

  numeric: 0.1
  outlier: 0.1

docs.title: Validation Errors
docs.text:
Dift provides clear threshold validation errors.

Example:

Thresholds must be between 0 and 1.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Recommended practices:

  • version-control threshold configs
  • separate thresholds by environment
  • use stricter thresholds in production
  • apply column overrides carefully
  • tune thresholds gradually
  • monitor alert frequency

docs.title: Usage Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to use Dift across common workflows, connectors, reporting systems, and automation pipelines.

docs.title: Basic Command Structure
docs.text:
dift [OLD_DATASET] [NEW_DATASET] [OPTIONS]

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
Dift compares:

  • local files
  • SQL tables
  • DuckDB tables
  • BigQuery tables
  • warehouse datasets

Most comparisons require:

  • old dataset
  • new dataset
  • row matching key

docs.title: CSV
docs.text: dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

docs.title: Parquet
docs.text: dift old.parquet new.parquet --key customer_id

docs.title: Excel
docs.text: dift old.xlsx new.xlsx --key customer_id

docs.title: JSON
docs.text: dift old.json new.json --key customer_id

docs.title: Drift Detection
docs.text:
Dift automatically detects:

  • schema drift
  • row-level changes
  • numeric drift
  • categorical drift
  • outlier spikes
  • null spikes
  • duplicate spikes

docs.title: Numeric Drift Thresholds
docs.text:
Control sensitivity using --threshold.

Default:

0.1

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --threshold 0.2

docs.title: Console Report
docs.text:
Default output:

dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

docs.title: JSON Report
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report json \
  --output report.json

docs.title: CSV Report
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report csv \
  --output report.csv

docs.title: Excel Report
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report excel \
  --output report.xlsx

docs.title: HTML Report
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --output report.html

docs.title: HTML Templates
docs.text:
Customize report styling:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --template enterprise \
  --output report.html

Available templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Output Directory Support
docs.text:
Automatically generate report filenames:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/

Generated files:

reports/
└── dift_report.html

docs.text: Dift supports reusable configuration files.

docs.text:

new_dataset: examples/new.csv
key: customer_id

Run:

docs.text:

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Dift supports environment variable interpolation.

Example:

new_dataset: ${NEW_DATASET}

Set variables:

export OLD_DATASET=data/old.csv
export NEW_DATASET=data/new.csv

Run:

├── old/
└── new/

docs.title: Run Batch Comparison
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --key customer_id

docs.title: Batch Reports
docs.text:
dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/

docs.title: Comparison History
docs.text:
Save comparison history:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --history

docs.title: View History
docs.text: dift history list

docs.title: Show History Entry
docs.text: dift history show 1

docs.title: Clear History
docs.text: dift history clear

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text:
Generate cron workflows:

dift schedule cron nightly-check

Example:

0 2 * * * dift profile run nightly-check --history

docs.title: Strict Exit Codes
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --strict-exit-codes
Exit Code Meaning 0 Low risk 1 Medium risk 2 High risk 3 Runtime error

docs.title: Quiet Mode
docs.text: dift old.csv new.csv --quiet

docs.title: Disable Colors
docs.text: dift old.csv new.csv --no-color

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Dift provides lightweight progress indicators during:

  • dataset loading
  • SQL extraction
  • warehouse queries
  • schema comparison
  • drift analysis
  • report generation

Useful for:

  • large datasets
  • warehouse comparisons
  • CI/CD monitoring
  • long-running workflows

dift duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb:customers_old \

docs.title: Connectors
docs.text:
Dift supports dataset comparison across local files, SQL databases, analytical warehouses, and future extensible connector systems.

This section documents all officially supported connectors and warehouse integrations.

docs.title: Local File Connectors
docs.text:
Dift supports:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
  • JSON

These are loaded directly into Polars DataFrames before comparison.

docs.title: SQL Database Connectors
docs.text:
Dift currently supports:

  • SQLite
  • PostgreSQL
  • MySQL

SQL support is powered by:

  • SQLAlchemy
  • Polars database integration

docs.title: Analytical Warehouse Connectors
docs.text:
Dift supports:

  • DuckDB
  • BigQuery
  • Redshift
  • Snowflake

These connectors enable warehouse validation and analytical comparison workflows.

docs.title: Connector Architecture
docs.text:
Dift uses a modular connector architecture built around:

  • shared reader interfaces
  • centralized reader registry
  • connector isolation
  • plugin preparation systems

This architecture enables future extensibility and third-party connector support.

docs.title: Connector Routing
docs.text:
Dataset sources are automatically routed using the centralized reader registry.

Example:

postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/db:customers

automatically resolves to:

SQLReader

docs.title: Connector Workflow
docs.text:
Internal loading flow:

CLI
  ↓
Reader Registry
  ↓
Connector Reader
  ↓
Polars DataFrame
  ↓
Comparison Engine

docs.title: Shared Connector Features
docs.text:
All connectors integrate with:

  • schema comparison
  • row comparison
  • drift detection
  • risk scoring
  • report generation
  • automation workflows
  • history tracking

docs.title: Supported Report Formats
docs.text:
Connectors support:

  • console reports
  • JSON reports
  • CSV reports
  • Excel reports
  • HTML reports

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Long-running connector workflows provide lightweight progress feedback.

Progress visibility includes:

  • dataset loading
  • warehouse extraction
  • query execution
  • comparison execution
  • report generation

docs.title: Authentication & Security
docs.text:
Authentication depends on connector type.

Examples:

Connector Authentication SQLite Local file access PostgreSQL Username/password MySQL Username/password BigQuery Google service account Snowflake Warehouse credentials Redshift Warehouse credentials

Future goals include:

docs.title: BigQuery Support
docs.text:
Dift supports direct dataset comparison using Google BigQuery.

BigQuery workflows enable:

  • cloud warehouse validation
  • analytical regression testing
  • production data quality checks
  • large-scale comparison workflows
  • scheduled warehouse monitoring
  • enterprise trust validation

docs.title: BigQuery URI Format
docs.text:
Dift uses the following BigQuery URI format:

bigquery://project.dataset.table

Example:

bigquery://acme-analytics.sales.orders

docs.title: Basic Comparison Example
docs.text:
dift bigquery://analytics.sales.customers_old \
     bigquery://analytics.sales.customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: Generate HTML Report
docs.text:
dift bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_old \
     bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_new \
     --key order_id \
     --report html \
     --output report.html

docs.title: Generate JSON Report
docs.text:
dift bigquery://analytics.sales.customers_old \
     bigquery://analytics.sales.customers_new \
     --key customer_id \
     --report json \
     --output report.json

docs.title: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
Install BigQuery support:

pip install google-cloud-bigquery db-dtypes

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Dift uses standard Google Cloud authentication.

Set your Google service account credentials:

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text: export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/service-account.json"

docs.title: Windows Git Bash
docs.text: export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="C:/path/to/service-account.json"

docs.title: PowerShell
docs.text: $env:GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="C:\path\to\service-account.json"

docs.title: Supported Features
docs.text:
BigQuery datasets support:

  • schema comparison
  • row comparison
  • drift detection
  • null analysis
  • duplicate analysis
  • risk scoring
  • batch workflows
  • comparison history
  • scheduled workflows

docs.title: Report Support
docs.text:
BigQuery workflows support:

  • console reports
  • JSON reports
  • CSV reports
  • Excel reports
  • HTML reports

docs.title: Internal Workflow
docs.text:
BigQuery datasets follow this workflow:

BigQuery
  ↓
Google BigQuery Client
  ↓
Polars DataFrame
  ↓
Dift Comparison Engine

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Large BigQuery workflows provide lightweight progress feedback during:

  • authentication
  • query execution
  • dataset extraction
  • comparison execution
  • report generation

docs.title: Production Warehouse Validation
docs.text:
dift bigquery://prod.sales.orders \
     bigquery://candidate.sales.orders

docs.title: ETL Regression Testing
docs.text:
dift bigquery://warehouse.v1.metrics \
     bigquery://warehouse.v2.metrics

docs.title: Analytics Quality Checks
docs.text:
dift bigquery://analytics.daily.customers_old \
     bigquery://analytics.daily.customers_new

docs.title: Security Recommendations
docs.text:
Recommended production practices:

  • use dedicated service accounts
  • avoid committing credentials
  • use environment variables
  • use least-privilege permissions
  • restrict dataset access

docs.title: Environment Variable Example
docs.text:
export BQ_OLD="bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_old"
export BQ_NEW="bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_new"

Then:

dift $BQ_OLD $BQ_NEW --key order_id

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Dift provides BigQuery-aware validation errors for:

  • missing BigQuery dependencies
  • invalid BigQuery URIs
  • authentication failures
  • missing datasets
  • missing tables
  • query execution failures

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• BigQuery access requires valid Google Cloud credentials.
  • BigQuery billing and permissions are managed through Google Cloud.
  • Datasets are loaded into Polars DataFrames before comparison.
  • Comparisons use the standard Dift comparison engine and reporting workflows.
  • Performance depends on dataset size and warehouse query execution time.

docs.text:

mysql+pymysql://user:password@host:3306/database:table_name

Example:

mysql+pymysql://admin:secret@localhost:3306/sales_db:customers

     --key customer_id

docs.title: Snowflake Support
docs.text:
Dift supports direct dataset comparison from Snowflake warehouses.

Snowflake workflows enable:

  • warehouse trust validation
  • production analytics QA
  • ETL verification
  • large-scale dataset comparison
  • scheduled warehouse monitoring
  • enterprise validation workflows

docs.title: Snowflake URI Format
docs.text:
Dift supports Snowflake connection strings in the following format:

snowflake://user:password@account/database/schema?warehouse=name:table_name

Example:

snowflake://admin:secret@acme-prod/sales/public?warehouse=compute_wh:orders

docs.title: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
Install Snowflake support:

pip install snowflake-sqlalchemy

docs.text: pip install sqlalchemy

docs.title: Basic Comparison Example
docs.text:
dift snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_old \
     snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_new \
     --key order_id

docs.title: Generate HTML Report
docs.text:
dift snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:customers_old \
     snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:customers_new \
     --key customer_id \
     --report html \
     --output report.html

docs.title: Generate JSON Report
docs.text:
dift snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_old \
     snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_new \
     --key order_id \
     --report json \
     --output report.json

  ↓
SQLAlchemy
  ↓
Polars DataFrame

     snowflake://candidate/customers

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Authentication is handled through Snowflake credentials in the connection string.

Example:

snowflake://username:password@account/database/schema?warehouse=name:table

docs.title: Security Recommendations
docs.text:
Recommended production practices:

  • avoid hardcoded credentials
  • use environment variables
  • use Snowflake role-based access control
  • use least-privilege warehouse accounts
  • rotate credentials regularly

docs.title: Dift Architecture
docs.text:
This document explains the internal architecture of Dift, including the comparison engine, reader system, reporting pipeline, configuration system, and future plugin preparation.

The goal of this document is to help contributors and maintainers understand how Dift is structured internally and how new capabilities can be added safely and consistently.

docs.title: Architecture Goals
docs.text:
Dift is designed around the following principles:

  • modularity
  • extensibility
  • predictable validation behavior
  • reusable comparison workflows
  • connector scalability
  • automation friendliness
  • plugin readiness
  • minimal core coupling

docs.title: CLI Layer
docs.text:
File:

dift/cli.py

Responsibilities:

  • parse CLI arguments
  • validate options
  • load configuration files
  • initialize comparisons
  • manage automation flags
  • trigger report generation
  • handle progress output
  • manage exit codes

The CLI layer acts as the orchestration layer for the entire platform.

docs.title: Configuration System
docs.text:
Key files:

dift/io/config_loader.py
dift/profiles.py
dift/thresholds.py
dift/schedules.py

Responsibilities:

  • YAML/TOML/JSON parsing
  • environment interpolation
  • reusable threshold policies
  • saved comparison profiles
  • scheduling configuration
  • configuration precedence resolution

Priority order:

CLI Arguments
→ Profiles
→ Config Files
→ Defaults

docs.title: Dataset Reader System
docs.text:
Key files:

dift/io/readers.py
dift/io/base_reader.py
dift/io/registry.py

Responsibilities:

  • dataset loading
  • connector routing
  • URI validation
  • connector abstraction
  • future plugin preparation

Supported sources:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel
  • JSON
  • DuckDB
  • SQL databases
  • BigQuery
  • warehouses

docs.title: Reader Registry Architecture
docs.text:
Dift uses a centralized registry architecture.

Example flow:

reader = registry.get_reader(source)
df = reader.read(source)

Benefits:

  • centralized routing
  • modular connectors
  • future plugin support
  • reduced coupling
  • cleaner connector isolation

docs.title: Base Reader Interface
docs.text:
All readers implement a shared interface:

class BaseReader:
    def can_handle(self, source: str) -> bool:
        ...

    def read(self, source: str):
        ...

This standardizes connector behavior across the platform.

docs.title: Connector Design Philosophy
docs.text:
Connectors are designed to be:

  • isolated
  • reusable
  • independently testable
  • plugin-safe
  • optionally loadable

This prepares Dift for future ecosystem expansion.

docs.title: Comparison Engine
docs.text:
Key file:

dift/core/comparator.py

Responsibilities:

  • coordinate dataset comparison
  • aggregate comparison results
  • execute diff modules
  • calculate risk
  • generate unified report models

The comparator acts as the central execution engine.

docs.title: Schema Comparison
docs.text:
Key file:

dift/core/schema_diff.py

Responsibilities:

  • added column detection
  • removed column detection
  • datatype changes
  • schema compatibility analysis

  • key-based matching
  • row count deltas

  • null spike detection

  • quality degradation analysis

docs.title: Drift Analysis
docs.text:

dift/core/stats_diff.py

Responsibilities:

  • numeric drift detection
  • categorical drift detection
  • outlier analysis
  • threshold evaluation
  • severity classification

docs.title: Numeric Drift
docs.text:
Numeric analysis includes:

  • mean shifts
  • standard deviation shifts
  • range changes
  • configurable thresholds

docs.title: Categorical Drift
docs.text:
Categorical analysis includes:

  • new values
  • removed values
  • frequency distribution shifts

docs.title: Outlier Analysis
docs.text:
Outlier analysis uses:

  • IQR detection

  • severity classification

docs.text:
Key file:

dift/core/risk.py

Responsibilities:

  • weighted risk calculation

  • risk normalization
  • final risk classification

Risk levels:

  • low
  • medium
  • high

docs.title: Plugin Preparation Architecture
docs.text:
This document explains how Dift is being internally prepared for future plugin-based connector and ecosystem expansion.

Although Dift does not yet support external plugins, the internal architecture has been intentionally designed to make future plugin support technically feasible without major rewrites.

docs.title: Why Plugin Preparation Matters
docs.text:
As Dift grows, users and organizations will want support for:

  • cloud platforms
  • enterprise warehouses
  • streaming systems
  • storage platforms
  • proprietary connectors
  • organization-specific integrations

Embedding every connector directly into the core codebase would eventually create:

  • tight coupling
  • dependency bloat
  • slower releases
  • maintenance complexity
  • unstable scaling

Plugin preparation helps avoid this.

docs.title: Long-Term Vision
docs.text:
Dift aims to support an ecosystem where connectors can evolve independently from the core engine.

Potential future ecosystem:

dift/plugins/
├── snowflake/
├── databricks/
├── kafka/
├── s3/
├── spark/
├── clickhouse/
└── custom/

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
Today, all connectors still live inside the core repository.

Examples:

dift/io/
├── sql_reader.py
├── duckdb_reader.py
└── bigquery_reader.py

However, the architecture has already been refactored to reduce coupling and prepare future separation.

docs.title: Architectural Goals
docs.text:
The plugin preparation work focuses on:

  • modular connector loading
  • connector isolation
  • dynamic registration
  • reusable interfaces
  • optional dependencies
  • future external packages
  • enterprise extensibility

docs.title: Core Design Principles
docs.text:
The plugin architecture preparation follows these principles:

  • connectors should be isolated
  • connectors should be optional
  • the comparison engine should remain connector-agnostic
  • readers should follow a shared contract
  • connector routing should be centralized
  • new connectors should require minimal core changes

docs.title: Current Foundations Already Implemented
docs.text: Several important architectural changes have already been completed.

docs.title: Reader Abstraction
docs.text:
Dift now uses a shared reader interface.

Example:

class BaseReader:
    def can_handle(self, source: str) -> bool:
        ...

    def read(self, source: str):
        ...

Benefits:

  • standardized connector behavior
  • reusable routing
  • simplified plugin contracts

docs.title: Centralized Registry System
docs.text:
Dift now uses a centralized reader registry.

Example:

registry.register(MyReader())
reader = registry.get_reader(source)

Benefits:

  • dynamic registration
  • centralized routing
  • future plugin discovery
  • connector prioritization

docs.title: Connector Isolation
docs.text:
Each connector now lives independently.

Examples:

sql_reader.py
duckdb_reader.py
bigquery_reader.py

Benefits:

  • isolated dependencies
  • independent testing
  • future package extraction

docs.title: Unified Dataset Contract
docs.text:
All readers return:

polars.DataFrame

This is extremely important.

The comparison engine never needs to understand:

  • SQLAlchemy
  • DuckDB
  • BigQuery
  • storage APIs
  • warehouse authentication

It only receives standardized DataFrames.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: This allows connectors to evolve independently while keeping the comparison engine stable.

docs.title: Dependency Isolation
docs.text:
Connectors use optional imports.

Example:

try:
    import duckdb
except ImportError:
    duckdb = None

Benefits:

  • lightweight installs
  • optional features
  • smaller dependency footprint
  • future plugin extraction

docs.title: Plugin-Safe Error Handling
docs.text:
Connectors now expose actionable errors.

Example:

Snowflake support requires:
  pip install snowflake-sqlalchemy

Benefits:

  • cleaner plugin UX
  • dependency guidance
  • safer optional loading

docs.title: Future Plugin Loading
docs.text:
Future architecture may support:

registry.load_plugins()

Potential behaviors:

  • auto-discovery
  • entry-point loading
  • optional registration
  • lazy imports

docs.title: Possible Future Plugin Structure
docs.text:
Potential future package layout:

dift-snowflake/
├── plugin.py
├── reader.py
├── auth.py
└── requirements.txt

docs.title: Possible Registration Workflow
docs.text:
Potential future behavior:

from dift_snowflake import SnowflakeReader

registry.register(SnowflakeReader())

docs.title: Why Dynamic Registration Matters
docs.text:
Dynamic registration allows:

  • third-party connectors
  • enterprise-only integrations
  • community-maintained plugins
  • experimentation without core changes

docs.title: Optional Connector Loading
docs.text:
Future connectors may become separately installable.

Examples:

pip install dift-snowflake
pip install dift-kafka
pip install dift-databricks

Benefits:

  • smaller core package
  • faster installs
  • reduced dependency conflicts
  • modular ecosystems

docs.title: Enterprise Connector Possibilities
docs.text:
Plugin preparation also supports future enterprise integrations.

Examples:

  • Snowflake
  • Databricks
  • S3
  • Kafka
  • Delta Lake
  • Iceberg
  • proprietary warehouses

docs.title: Connector Lifecycle Preparation
docs.text:
Future plugins may support:

  • initialization hooks
  • teardown hooks
  • authentication providers
  • configuration validation
  • capability inspection

Potential future interface:

class Plugin:
    def initialize(self):
        ...

    def shutdown(self):
        ...

docs.title: Plugin Metadata (Future)
docs.text:
Future plugin metadata may include:

class PluginMetadata:
    name: str
    version: str
    supported_sources: list[str]
    dependencies: list[str]

This would support:

  • plugin discovery
  • compatibility checks
  • UI integrations
  • debugging workflows

docs.title: Plugin Capability Discovery
docs.text:
Potential future capability inspection:

registry.list_capabilities()

Example output:

- SQL databases
- BigQuery
- Snowflake
- Kafka
- S3

docs.title: Why Plugin Isolation Is Important
docs.text:
Isolation reduces risk.

A broken connector should not:

  • crash the core engine
  • break unrelated connectors
  • block local dataset comparisons

docs.title: Connector Failure Philosophy
docs.text:
Connector failures should be:

  • isolated
  • actionable
  • recoverable
  • dependency-aware

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
Dift does NOT yet support:

  • external plugins
  • auto-discovery
  • plugin installation APIs
  • entry-point registration
  • runtime plugin loading

However, the internal architecture is now being prepared for these features.

docs.title: Why Preparation Happens Early
docs.text:
Architectural preparation is easier before ecosystem growth becomes large.

Retrofitting plugin systems later becomes significantly more difficult.

docs.title: Current Connector Flow
docs.text:
Current behavior:

CLI
  ↓
Registry
  ↓
Reader
  ↓
Polars DataFrame
  ↓
Comparison Engine

This already resembles a plugin-friendly architecture.

docs.title: Future Plugin Flow
docs.text:
Potential future architecture:

CLI
  ↓
Plugin Loader
  ↓
Registry
  ↓
External Readers
  ↓
Polars DataFrame

docs.title: Stability Goals
docs.text:
Plugin preparation should NOT compromise:

  • comparison stability
  • report consistency
  • risk scoring
  • existing workflows

Core functionality remains stable and connector-agnostic.

docs.title: Benefits of Plugin Preparation
docs.text:
This architecture enables future:

  • enterprise ecosystems
  • community integrations
  • connector marketplaces
  • warehouse ecosystems
  • streaming integrations
  • cloud-native workflows

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
The plugin preparation architecture prioritizes:

  • long-term scalability
  • loose coupling
  • modularity
  • maintainability
  • ecosystem growth

docs.text:

docs.title: Reader Registry System
docs.text:
This document explains Dift’s centralized dataset reader registry architecture, connector routing system, and extensibility model.

The reader registry is a foundational part of Dift’s internal architecture because it enables scalable connector growth while keeping the comparison engine connector-agnostic.

docs.title: Why the Reader Registry Exists
docs.text:
Earlier versions of Dift handled dataset routing directly inside a single loading workflow.

As connector support expanded, this created several architectural problems:

  • connector logic became tightly coupled
  • routing logic became difficult to maintain
  • validation behavior became duplicated
  • adding connectors required core modifications
  • plugin preparation became difficult

The reader registry architecture solves these issues by introducing a centralized routing and registration system.

docs.title: Design Goals
docs.text:
The reader registry system was designed to provide:

  • centralized connector routing
  • modular dataset readers
  • reusable validation workflows
  • dynamic reader registration
  • future plugin preparation
  • connector isolation
  • scalable connector architecture

docs.title: Core Files
docs.text:
The registry architecture is implemented primarily in:

dift/io/
├── base_reader.py
├── registry.py
├── readers.py
├── sql_reader.py
├── duckdb_reader.py
└── bigquery_reader.py

docs.title: Base Reader Interface
docs.text:
File:

dift/io/base_reader.py

All dataset readers implement a shared interface.

Example:

class BaseReader:
    def can_handle(self, source: str) -> bool:
        ...

    def read(self, source: str):
        ...

docs.title: Why a Shared Interface Matters
docs.text:
The shared interface standardizes:

  • connector behavior
  • routing logic
  • dataset loading contracts
  • validation expectations

This allows the registry to treat all readers consistently.

docs.title: Reader Responsibilities
docs.text:
Each reader is responsible for:

  • determining whether it supports a source
  • validating connector input
  • loading datasets
  • raising actionable errors

Readers should NOT:

  • perform comparisons
  • generate reports
  • calculate risk
  • handle CLI orchestration

docs.title: Reader Registry
docs.text:
File:

dift/io/registry.py

The registry acts as the central connector routing system.

Responsibilities include:

  • registering readers
  • discovering compatible readers
  • prioritizing readers
  • centralizing routing logic

docs.title: Registry Example
docs.text:
Example registration:

registry = ReaderRegistry()

registry.register(LocalFileReader())
registry.register(SQLReader())
registry.register(DuckDBReader())

docs.title: Reader Resolution Example
docs.text:
Example routing workflow:

reader = registry.get_reader(source)
df = reader.read(source)

docs.title: Reader Discovery Flow
docs.text:
Current routing flow:

Source Input
      ↓
Registry Iteration
      ↓
Reader.can_handle()
      ↓
Compatible Reader
      ↓
Reader.read()

docs.title: Current Built-In Readers
docs.text:

Reader Purpose LocalFileReader Local files DuckDBReader DuckDB databases SQLReader SQL databases BigQueryReader BigQuery warehouses

Supported systems include:

  • SQLite

docs.title: Unified Dataset Contract
docs.text:
All readers return:

polars.DataFrame

This is extremely important because the comparison engine remains completely connector-agnostic.

The comparison engine never needs to understand:

  • SQLAlchemy
  • DuckDB
  • warehouse APIs
  • cloud authentication
  • connector-specific behavior

docs.title: Why Connector Isolation Matters
docs.text:
Connector isolation improves:

  • maintainability
  • testability
  • scalability
  • plugin preparation
  • dependency management

docs.title: Validation Philosophy
docs.text:
Readers are responsible for actionable validation behavior.

Examples include:

  • unsupported format guidance
  • invalid URI guidance
  • missing dependency guidance
  • connector troubleshooting hints

docs.title: Good Validation Example
docs.text:
PostgreSQL support requires psycopg2.

Install it with:
  pip install psycopg2-binary

docs.title: Poor Validation Example
docs.text: ValueError: failed

docs.title: Reader Prioritization
docs.text:
Reader registration order matters.

Example:

registry.register(SQLReader())
registry.register(LocalFileReader())

Specialized readers should generally be registered before generic readers.

docs.title: Why Prioritization Exists
docs.text:
Some source patterns may overlap.

Prioritization ensures:

  • deterministic routing
  • predictable behavior
  • extensibility safety

docs.title: Centralized Routing Benefits
docs.text:
Without a registry:

CLI
 └── Large conditional routing logic

With a registry:

CLI
 └── Registry
      └── Readers

Benefits include:

  • cleaner architecture
  • modular connectors
  • future scalability

docs.title: Dynamic Registration
docs.text:
Readers can be registered dynamically.

Example:

registry.register(MyCustomReader())

This is foundational for future plugin support.

docs.text:
Current workflow:
