docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
This document explains the release workflow, versioning philosophy, packaging process, and maintenance workflow used in Dift.

The goal of the release process is to ensure that Dift releases remain:

  • stable
  • reproducible
  • well-tested
  • backward compatible
  • automation friendly
  • professionally documented

docs.title: Release Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift follows a release philosophy centered around:

  • incremental improvements
  • stability-first development
  • developer experience
  • automation compatibility
  • extensibility preparation

Releases should improve the platform without introducing unnecessary breaking changes.

docs.title: Current Versioning Strategy
docs.text:
Dift currently uses semantic-style versioning:

MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH

Example:

0.6.0

docs.title: Version Meaning
docs.text: Part Meaning MAJOR Significant architectural changes MINOR New features and improvements PATCH Bug fixes and small corrections

docs.title: Current Development Phase
docs.text:
Dift is currently in the:

0.x.x

phase.

This means:

  • architecture is still evolving
  • features are expanding rapidly
  • internal APIs may still evolve

However, stability and backward compatibility are still strongly prioritized.

docs.title: Typical Release Workflow
docs.text:
The standard release process is:

Feature Development
        ↓
Testing
        ↓
Documentation Updates
        ↓
Version Bump
        ↓
Release Notes
        ↓
Git Tag
        ↓
PyPI Release
        ↓
GitHub Release

docs.title: Step 1 — Feature Development
docs.text:
Features are typically developed through:

  • isolated branches
  • pull requests
  • incremental commits

Recommended branch naming:

feature/add-html-template-system
fix/sql-validation-errors
refactor/reader-registry

docs.title: Step 2 — Run Tests
docs.text:
Before releasing:

pytest

All tests should pass successfully.

docs.title: Step 3 — Run Linting
docs.text:
Run lint checks:

ruff check .

docs.title: Step 4 — Run Type Checking
docs.text:
Run static typing checks:

mypy dift

docs.title: Step 5 — Manual Validation
docs.text:
Manual workflows should also be tested.

Examples:

dift examples/old.csv examples/new.csv --key customer_id

docs.title: Important Manual Validation Areas
docs.text:
Before release, verify:

  • CLI help output
  • report generation
  • output directory support
  • connector workflows
  • config loading
  • scheduling workflows
  • progress indicators

docs.title: Step 6 — Update Version Numbers
docs.text:
Update version references in:

pyproject.toml
README.md
dift/__init__.py   (if applicable)

Example:

0.5.0 → 0.6.0

docs.title: Step 7 — Update Documentation
docs.text:
Update:

  • README
  • release notes
  • roadmap
  • developer docs
  • examples

Documentation updates are considered part of the release itself.

docs.title: Step 8 — Create Release Notes
docs.text:
Release notes should summarize:

  • new features
  • fixes
  • architectural improvements
  • contributor credits
  • migration notes

docs.title: Example Release Structure
docs.text:
# What's New in v0.6.0

## New Features
- SQL connectors
- BigQuery support
- Reader registry system

## Improvements
- Better validation errors
- Progress indicators
- Improved HTML reports

docs.title: Step 9 — Build Distribution Packages
docs.text:
Build packages:

python -m build

This generates:

dist/
├── *.whl
└── *.tar.gz

docs.title: Step 10 — Verify Package
docs.text:
Verify generated packages:

twine check dist/*

docs.title: Step 11 — Publish to PyPI
docs.text:
Upload to PyPI:

twine upload dist/*

docs.title: Step 12 — Create Git Tag
docs.text:
Create version tag:

git tag v0.6.0
git push origin v0.6.0

docs.title: Step 13 — GitHub Release
docs.text:
Create a GitHub release using:

  • version tag
  • release notes
  • changelog summary

docs.title: Recommended Release Checklist
docs.text:
Before releasing:

[ ] All tests pass
[ ] Lint checks pass
[ ] Type checks pass
[ ] Documentation updated
[ ] README updated
[ ] Version bumped
[ ] Release notes written
[ ] Manual workflows validated
[ ] Package builds successfully

docs.title: Backward Compatibility Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift strongly prioritizes:

  • stable CLI behavior
  • stable report schemas
  • stable automation workflows

Breaking changes should be minimized.

docs.title: JSON Schema Stability
docs.text:
JSON reports are especially important because they may be consumed by:

  • CI/CD systems
  • APIs
  • automation pipelines
  • integrations

Schema-breaking changes should be handled carefully.

docs.title: Automation Stability
docs.text:
Release validation should ensure:

  • strict exit codes still behave correctly
  • quiet mode remains stable
  • scheduled workflows still work
  • progress indicators remain automation-safe

docs.title: Dependency Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift aims to keep core dependencies:

  • lightweight
  • stable
  • optional when possible

Connector-specific dependencies should remain isolated.

docs.title: Optional Dependency Strategy
docs.text:
Examples:

pip install sqlalchemy
pip install duckdb
pip install google-cloud-bigquery

This helps reduce unnecessary install bloat.

docs.title: Connector Stability
docs.text:
Connector releases should preserve:

  • URI formats
  • routing behavior
  • validation clarity
  • dependency guidance

docs.title: Release Documentation Philosophy
docs.text:
Documentation should evolve alongside architecture.

Major features should never ship undocumented.

docs.title: Contributor Release Expectations
docs.text:
Before merging major features:

  • tests should exist
  • documentation should exist
  • CLI UX should be validated
  • error messaging should be reviewed

docs.title: Recommended Release Frequency
docs.text:
Dift currently favors:

Feature-focused minor releases

instead of overly frequent micro releases.

This helps maintain:

  • release quality
  • architectural consistency
  • manageable documentation

docs.title: Internal Refactor Policy
docs.text:
Internal architecture improvements are encouraged when they:

  • reduce coupling
  • improve extensibility
  • simplify maintenance
  • prepare future scaling

But they should avoid unnecessary user-facing disruption.

docs.title: Roadmap Alignment
docs.text:
Releases should align with the public roadmap.

Example areas:

  • connectors
  • plugin preparation
  • automation workflows
  • enterprise reporting
  • warehouse integrations

docs.title: Future Release Goals
docs.text:
Future release infrastructure may include:

  • automated GitHub Actions publishing
  • automated changelog generation
  • release validation pipelines
  • plugin compatibility checks
  • integration test matrices

docs.title: Potential Future CI Pipeline
docs.text:
Example future release flow:

GitHub Actions
    ↓
Run Tests
    ↓
Run Linting
    ↓
Build Package
    ↓
Publish to PyPI
    ↓
Create GitHub Release

docs.title: Release Quality Principles
docs.text:
Every Dift release should aim to improve:

  • reliability
  • usability
  • extensibility
  • trust validation workflows
  • developer experience

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
The Dift release process prioritizes:

  • stability
  • reproducibility
  • maintainability

docs.title: Report System
docs.text:
This document explains the internal report architecture of Dift, including report models, rendering workflows, export systems, and future extensibility goals.

The reporting system is one of the core pillars of Dift because it transforms raw comparison results into actionable, human-readable, and automation-friendly outputs.

docs.title: Reporting Goals
docs.text:
The Dift reporting system is designed to provide:

  • consistent report structures
  • reusable rendering logic
  • multiple export formats
  • automation-friendly outputs
  • human-readable summaries
  • enterprise-ready exports
  • extensible rendering pipelines

docs.title: Supported Report Formats
docs.text:
Dift currently supports:

Format Purpose Console Interactive CLI usage JSON Machine-readable workflows CSV Lightweight summaries Excel Enterprise spreadsheet workflows HTML Visual dashboard reporting

docs.title: Report Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Comparison Engine
        ↓
Report Models
        ↓
Renderer
        ↓
Output File

docs.title: Core Report Directory
docs.text:
dift/reports/
├── console_report.py
├── json_report.py
├── csv_report.py
├── excel_report.py
├── html_report.py
└── models.py

docs.title: Report Pipeline
docs.text:
The reporting workflow follows these stages:

  1. comparison execution
  2. result aggregation
  3. report model creation
  4. renderer selection
  5. report generation
  6. output export

docs.title: Report Models
docs.text:
File:

dift/reports/models.py

This file defines the shared internal report schema used by all report formats.

The report model acts as the:

Single source of truth

for report structure.

docs.title: Why Shared Models Matter
docs.text:
Without shared models:

  • report formats drift apart
  • inconsistent outputs emerge
  • features become duplicated
  • testing becomes difficult

Shared models ensure:

  • consistency
  • predictable serialization
  • reusable rendering
  • easier maintenance

docs.title: Current Report Structure
docs.text:
Dift reports currently contain:

{
  "metadata": {},
  "summary": {},
  "schema": {},
  "rows": {},
  "quality": {},
  "numeric": {},
  "categorical": {}
}

docs.title: Metadata Section
docs.text:
The metadata section includes:

  • tool name
  • version
  • execution timestamps
  • report type
  • runtime information

Example:

{
  "tool": "dift",
  "version": "0.6.0"
}

docs.title: Summary Section
docs.text:
Contains high-level comparison results.

Examples:

  • row counts
  • risk levels
  • warnings
  • drift summaries

docs.title: Schema Section
docs.text:
Contains schema comparison information.

Examples:

  • added columns
  • removed columns
  • datatype changes

docs.title: Row Section
docs.text:
Contains row-level comparison results.

Examples:

  • added rows
  • removed rows
  • row deltas

docs.title: Quality Section
docs.text:
Contains dataset quality analysis.

Examples:

  • null spikes
  • duplicate spikes
  • severity changes

docs.title: Numeric Drift Section
docs.text:
Contains numeric drift analysis.

Examples:

  • mean shifts
  • standard deviation drift
  • range changes

docs.title: Categorical Drift Section
docs.text:
Contains categorical drift analysis.

Examples:

  • new values
  • removed values
  • frequency distribution shifts

docs.title: Console Reports
docs.text:

console_report.py

  • Rich terminal rendering
  • risk highlighting
  • warning summaries

docs.title: Console Report Goals
docs.text:
Console reports prioritize:

  • readability

  • lightweight rendering

report = compare_datasets(...)

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
This document explains the testing philosophy, testing architecture, and recommended testing workflows used in Dift.

Testing is a critical part of Dift because the project focuses on:

  • data trust validation
  • regression detection
  • automation workflows
  • connector reliability
  • report consistency

Dift aims to maintain predictable and stable behavior across all supported workflows.

docs.title: Testing Goals
docs.text:
The Dift testing strategy focuses on:

  • preventing regressions
  • validating comparison correctness
  • ensuring connector reliability
  • maintaining report consistency
  • validating CLI workflows
  • protecting automation behavior
  • enabling safe refactoring

docs.title: Testing Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift emphasizes:

  • readable tests
  • isolated failures
  • realistic workflows
  • automation-safe validation
  • actionable failure output

Tests should behave like real user workflows whenever possible.

docs.title: Test Directory Structure
docs.text:
tests/
├── test_cli.py
├── test_cli_errors.py
├── test_sql_reader.py
├── test_connector_integration.py
├── test_warehouse_mocking.py
├── test_json_report.py
├── test_excel_report.py
├── test_html_report.py
├── test_batch.py
├── test_history.py
└── ...

docs.title: Core Testing Areas
docs.text:
Dift tests focus on:

Area Purpose CLI Tests User workflow validation Reader Tests Connector reliability Report Tests Output consistency Integration Tests End-to-end workflows Validation Tests Error clarity Automation Tests CI/CD compatibility

docs.title: Primary Testing Framework
docs.text:
Dift uses:

pytest

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Run all tests:

pytest

Run a specific file:

pytest tests/test_cli.py

Run a specific test:

pytest tests/test_cli.py::test_cli_help_runs

docs.title: Test Environment
docs.text:
Typical environment:

Python 3.10+
pytest

Dift is tested across multiple workflows including:

  • local datasets
  • warehouse connectors
  • batch comparisons
  • report generation
  • automation execution

docs.title: CLI Testing
docs.text:
CLI tests validate:

  • argument parsing
  • help output
  • validation behavior
  • automation flags
  • report generation
  • progress indicators

Example:

result = runner.invoke(compare_app, ["--help"])

assert result.exit_code == 0

docs.title: Subprocess CLI Testing
docs.text:
Some tests use:

subprocess.run(...)

This validates:

  • real shell behavior
  • terminal compatibility
  • actual CLI execution

docs.title: Why Subprocess Testing Matters
docs.text:
Typer runner tests are useful, but subprocess tests validate:

  • true command execution
  • real environment behavior
  • packaging compatibility

docs.title: Reader Testing
docs.text:
Reader tests validate:

  • URI parsing
  • routing behavior
  • connector validation
  • dependency guidance
  • error handling

docs.title: SQL Reader Tests
docs.text:
Examples:

  • valid SQL URI parsing
  • invalid URI rejection
  • missing table handling
  • dependency guidance
  • query execution

docs.title: Warehouse Mock Testing
docs.text:
Warehouse tests use mocking to avoid:

  • real cloud credentials
  • billing costs
  • external dependencies

Examples:

  • BigQuery
  • Snowflake
  • Redshift

docs.title: Why Warehouse Mocking Matters
docs.text:
Warehouse mocking enables:

  • deterministic tests
  • fast execution
  • offline development
  • reproducible behavior

docs.title: Validation Testing
docs.text:
Validation tests ensure errors remain:

  • actionable
  • readable
  • consistent

Examples:

  • missing dataset guidance
  • invalid URI guidance
  • unsupported format guidance
  • dependency installation hints

docs.title: Example Validation Test
docs.text: assert "Supported local file types" in combined_output

docs.title: Report Testing
docs.text:
Report tests validate:

  • serialization consistency
  • output generation
  • schema stability
  • file creation

docs.title: JSON Report Testing
docs.text:
JSON report tests are especially important because JSON outputs are often consumed by:

  • CI/CD systems
  • APIs
  • automation workflows
  • downstream tooling

docs.title: JSON Regression Protection
docs.text:
Tests verify:

  • required fields exist
  • deprecated fields remain absent
  • schema stability is preserved

Example:

assert "metadata" in report
assert "summary" in report

docs.title: Excel Report Testing
docs.text:
Excel tests validate:

  • workbook generation
  • worksheet structure
  • formatting consistency

docs.title: HTML Report Testing
docs.text:
HTML tests validate:

  • template rendering
  • report creation
  • dashboard structure

docs.title: Batch Workflow Testing
docs.text:
Batch tests validate:

  • directory traversal
  • dataset matching
  • partial failure handling
  • report generation

docs.title: History System Testing
docs.text:
History tests validate:

  • history persistence
  • JSONL writing
  • listing behavior
  • cleanup workflows

docs.title: Automation Workflow Testing
docs.text:
Automation-focused tests validate:

  • strict exit codes
  • quiet mode
  • no-color mode
  • cron generation
  • scheduled execution

docs.title: Progress Indicator Testing
docs.text:
Progress testing ensures:

  • output remains readable
  • automation workflows stay stable
  • progress output does not corrupt reports

docs.title: Connector Integration Testing
docs.text:
Integration tests validate:

  • reader routing
  • registry behavior
  • connector compatibility
  • shared loading contracts

docs.title: Registry Testing
docs.text:
Registry tests validate:

  • dynamic registration
  • reader prioritization
  • source routing
  • extensibility behavior

docs.title: Plugin Preparation Testing
docs.text:
Plugin preparation tests validate:

  • reader isolation
  • shared interfaces
  • modular loading behavior

docs.title: Cross-Format Consistency Testing
docs.text:
Dift aims to ensure all report formats represent the same comparison result.

Tests help validate:

  • consistent risk levels
  • matching warning counts
  • stable summary metrics

docs.title: Temporary File Testing
docs.text:
Dift frequently uses:

tmp_path

Example:

def test_report_generation(tmp_path):

Benefits:

  • isolated execution
  • safe filesystem usage
  • reproducible tests

docs.title: Fixture Usage
docs.text:
Fixtures are used for:

  • sample datasets
  • temporary databases
  • reusable workflows

docs.title: Example Fixture Workflow
docs.text: def test_cli(sample_csv_files):

docs.title: Mocking Philosophy
docs.text:
Mocking is primarily used for:

  • warehouses
  • cloud connectors
  • expensive external systems

Dift prefers real execution whenever practical.

docs.title: Error Message Stability
docs.text:
Dift intentionally tests exact error wording in some cases.

Why?

Because actionable validation UX is considered a core feature.

docs.title: Performance Philosophy
docs.text:
Tests should remain:

  • deterministic
  • lightweight
  • reproducible

Large-scale benchmarking is intentionally separated from standard CI workflows.

docs.title: Linting
docs.text:
Run linting:

ruff check .

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text:
Run type checks:

mypy dift

docs.title: Recommended Development Workflow
docs.text:
Before opening a PR:

pytest
ruff check .
mypy dift

docs.title: Contributor Expectations
docs.text:
Contributors should:

  • add tests for new features
  • preserve backward compatibility
  • avoid breaking automation workflows
  • maintain report consistency

docs.title: Good Test Characteristics
docs.text:
Good tests should be:

  • readable
  • focused
  • deterministic
  • isolated
  • descriptive

docs.title: Bad Test Characteristics
docs.text:
Avoid tests that are:

  • overly coupled
  • flaky
  • network-dependent
  • environment-specific
  • difficult to debug

docs.title: Regression Prevention Philosophy
docs.text:
Dift heavily values regression prevention because:

  • data trust tooling must remain stable
  • automation workflows depend on predictable behavior
  • connector UX must remain consistent

docs.title: CI/CD Readiness
docs.text:
The test architecture is designed for:

  • GitHub Actions
  • Jenkins
  • GitLab CI
  • Airflow workflows
  • scheduled validation pipelines

docs.title: Future Testing Areas
docs.text:
Planned future testing improvements:

  • plugin compatibility testing
  • distributed execution testing
  • performance benchmarking
  • snapshot testing
  • visual HTML regression testing

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
The Dift testing architecture prioritizes:

  • reliability
  • stability
  • reproducibility
  • maintainability
  • automation compatibility

docs.title: Dift v0.1.0 Release Notes
docs.text: Release Date: Apr 26, 2026

docs.title: Dift v0.1.0
docs.text:
Initial public release of Dift — an open-source CLI platform for dataset comparison, drift detection, and data trust validation.

This release establishes the foundation of the Dift comparison engine, including schema comparison, row-level validation, quality analysis, and risk scoring workflows.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
Dift v0.1.0 introduces:

  • dataset comparison workflows
  • schema change detection
  • row-level comparison
  • null and duplicate analysis
  • risk scoring
  • console reporting
  • JSON reporting
  • CSV reporting
  • Excel reporting
  • HTML reporting
  • multi-format dataset support
  • CLI-based comparison workflows

docs.title: Dataset Comparison Engine
docs.text:
Core comparison capabilities include:

  • row count comparison
  • added row detection
  • removed row detection
  • key-based matching
  • schema validation

docs.title: Schema Comparison
docs.text:
Dift can detect:

  • added columns
  • removed columns
  • datatype mismatches

docs.title: Quality Validation
docs.text:
Initial quality analysis includes:

  • null spike detection
  • duplicate spike detection
  • quality degradation warnings

docs.title: Risk Scoring
docs.text:
Dift introduces a built-in risk scoring engine.

Initial risk levels:

  • low
  • medium
  • high

Risk scoring is based on:

  • schema changes
  • row differences
  • quality degradation

docs.title: Supported Dataset Formats
docs.text:
Initial supported formats:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
  • JSON

docs.title: Report Formats
docs.text:
Supported report outputs:

  • console report
  • JSON report
  • CSV summary report
  • Excel workbook report
  • HTML report

docs.title: HTML Reports
docs.text:
Initial HTML reporting support includes:

  • summary dashboards
  • warning sections
  • risk visibility
  • dataset metrics

docs.title: Excel Reports
docs.text:
Excel reports include:

  • summary sheets
  • schema comparison sheets
  • quality summaries

docs.title: JSON Reports
docs.text:
JSON reports support:

  • machine-readable workflows
  • automation pipelines
  • downstream integrations

docs.title: CLI Workflows
docs.text:
Example usage:

dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

Generate JSON report:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --key customer_id \
  --report json \
  --output report.json

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
╭─────────────────────────╮
│ Dift Dataset Comparison │
│ Risk Level: MEDIUM      │
╰─────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Initial Project Structure
docs.text:
dift/
├── cli.py
├── core/
├── io/
├── reports/
└── utils/

docs.title: Technical Foundations
docs.text:
Dift v0.1.0 establishes the initial architecture for:

  • modular comparison workflows
  • report rendering
  • reusable risk scoring
  • extensible dataset readers

docs.title: Supported Python Versions
docs.text: Python 3.10+

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install from PyPI:

pip install dift-cli

Verify installation:

dift --help

docs.title: Development Tooling
docs.text:
Development workflows include:

pytest
ruff check .

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
Initial release limitations:

  • local datasets only
  • no SQL database connectors
  • no warehouse integrations
  • no batch workflows
  • no saved profiles
  • no scheduling workflows

These capabilities are planned for future releases.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Dift aims to become the open-source standard for:

  • dataset regression testing
  • data drift monitoring
  • ML dataset validation
  • warehouse trust checks
  • automated data quality validation

docs.title: Thank You
docs.text:
Thank you to everyone supporting the early development of Dift.

This release marks the beginning of the Dift ecosystem and future platform expansion.

docs.title: Dift v0.2.1 Release Notes
docs.text: Release Date: Apr 28, 2026

docs.title: Dift v0.2.1
docs.text:
Dift v0.2.1 focuses on improving usability, report quality, dataset validation workflows, and overall CLI stability.

This release strengthens the foundation established in v0.1.0 by improving comparison visibility, report consistency, and user experience across local dataset workflows.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
Dift v0.2.1 introduces:

  • improved comparison reporting
  • enhanced console output
  • stronger validation workflows
  • improved report consistency
  • better dataset handling
  • improved risk visibility
  • CLI workflow refinements
  • stability improvements

docs.title: Improved Console Reports
docs.text:
Console reports now provide clearer visibility into:

  • schema changes
  • row differences
  • quality warnings
  • overall risk levels

The terminal output is now easier to scan and interpret during comparisons.

docs.title: Enhanced Risk Visibility
docs.text:
Risk summaries have been improved to make risky dataset changes easier to identify quickly.

Examples include:

  • clearer warning sections
  • improved severity presentation
  • better comparison summaries

docs.title: Improved HTML Reports
docs.text:
HTML reporting received several usability improvements:

  • cleaner layouts
  • improved section organization
  • better warning visibility
  • improved readability

docs.title: Improved Excel Reports
docs.text:
Excel reports now include:

  • improved worksheet formatting
  • cleaner summary sections
  • better comparison organization

docs.title: Better JSON Report Consistency
docs.text:
JSON reports were improved for:

  • cleaner serialization
  • more predictable structure
  • automation compatibility

This improves downstream integrations and machine-readable workflows.

docs.title: Validation Improvements
docs.text:
Validation workflows were improved across the CLI.

Enhancements include:

  • clearer missing dataset handling
  • improved unsupported format errors
  • better user guidance
  • improved validation consistency

docs.title: Dataset Handling Improvements
docs.text:
Dataset loading workflows were refined for:

  • improved reliability
  • cleaner error handling
  • more stable comparison execution

docs.title: CLI Improvements
docs.text:
CLI usability improvements include:

  • better help output
  • improved command guidance
  • clearer report workflows

docs.title: Stability Improvements
docs.text:
This release includes multiple internal improvements focused on:

  • comparison reliability
  • report generation stability
  • error handling consistency
  • maintainability

docs.title: Supported Dataset Formats

docs.title: Dift v0.3.0 Release Notes
docs.text: Release Date: May 3, 2026

docs.title: Dift v0.3.0
docs.text:
Dift v0.3.0 introduces major improvements focused on reporting workflows, configuration-driven execution, reusable validation settings, and developer experience improvements.

This release significantly expands Dift beyond simple dataset comparison into a more reusable and automation-friendly validation platform.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
Dift v0.3.0 introduces:

  • reusable configuration files
  • JSON configuration support
  • TOML configuration support
  • YAML configuration support
  • improved JSON reports
  • CSV summary reporting
  • Excel report improvements
  • HTML report improvements
  • reusable threshold configurations
  • column-level threshold overrides
  • environment-based configurations
  • environment variable support
  • output directory support
  • improved validation errors
  • stronger CLI workflows

docs.title: Configuration File Support
docs.text:
Dift now supports reusable configuration files.

Supported formats:

  • YAML
  • TOML
  • JSON

This allows teams to standardize and reuse comparison workflows.

docs.title: Example YAML Config
docs.text:
old_dataset: examples/old.csv
new_dataset: examples/new.csv
key: customer_id
threshold: 0.1
report: html

Run using:

dift --config config.yaml

docs.title: TOML Configuration Support
docs.text:
Example:

old_dataset = "examples/old.csv"
new_dataset = "examples/new.csv"
key = "customer_id"
report = "json"

docs.title: JSON Configuration Support
docs.text:
Example:

{
  "old_dataset": "examples/old.csv",
  "new_dataset": "examples/new.csv",
  "key": "customer_id",
  "report": "csv"
}

docs.title: Dataset Paths Inside Configs
docs.text:
Dataset paths can now be fully defined inside config files.

This enables cleaner automation workflows:

dift --config config_with_datasets.yaml

docs.title: CLI Override Support
docs.text:
CLI arguments now override config values.

Priority order:

CLI arguments > Config values > Defaults

This enables flexible workflow customization.

docs.title: Reusable Threshold Configurations
docs.text:
Dift now supports reusable threshold policies.

Thresholds can be configured globally or per-column.

docs.title: Global Threshold Example
docs.text:
thresholds:
  numeric: 0.1
  categorical: 0.2
  outlier: 0.15

docs.title: Column-Level Threshold Overrides
docs.text:
Example:

columns:
  revenue:
    numeric: 0.05

  segment:
    categorical: 0.3

This enables highly customized validation behavior.

docs.title: Environment-Based Configurations
docs.text:
Dift now supports reusable environment workflows.

Example:

environments:
  development:
    threshold: 0.2

  production:
    threshold: 0.05

Run using:

dift --config config_env.yaml --env production

docs.title: Environment Variable Support
docs.text:
Dift now supports environment variable interpolation inside config files.

Example:

old_dataset: ${OLD_DATASET}
new_dataset: ${NEW_DATASET}

This improves CI/CD and secret-management preparation.

docs.title: Output Directory Support
docs.text:
Reports can now be written directly to output directories.

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report json \
  --output-dir reports/

Dift automatically generates report filenames.

docs.title: Auto-Generated Report Names
docs.text:
Examples:

dift_report.json
dift_report.csv
dift_report.xlsx
dift_report.html

docs.title: Improved JSON Report Structure
docs.text:
JSON reports were redesigned for:

  • cleaner automation support
  • improved consistency
  • future extensibility

New report sections include:

  • metadata
  • summary
  • schema
  • rows
  • quality
  • numeric
  • categorical

docs.title: Metadata Support
docs.text:
JSON reports now include metadata such as:

  • tool name
  • version
  • report type

Example:

"metadata": {
  "tool": "dift",
  "version": "0.3.0"
}

docs.title: Better CSV Summary Reports
docs.text: CSV reporting now provides improved summary consistency for automation workflows and lightweight monitoring.

docs.title: Improved Excel Reports
docs.text:
Excel reports received multiple improvements:

  • better formatting
  • improved worksheet organization
  • cleaner readability
  • improved summaries

docs.title: Improved HTML Reports
docs.text:
HTML reports now include:

  • better layouts
  • improved summaries
  • cleaner warning visibility
  • improved readability

docs.title: Better Validation Errors
docs.text:
Validation workflows were significantly improved.

Examples include:

  • clearer missing dataset guidance
  • improved unsupported file type errors
  • actionable configuration validation

docs.title: Example Unsupported File Error
docs.text:
Unsupported dataset type '.txt'.

Supported local file types:
.csv, .json, .parquet, .xlsx

docs.title: Improved CLI UX
docs.text:
CLI workflows were improved through:

  • clearer help output
  • better command guidance
  • improved validation behavior
  • cleaner execution workflows

docs.title: Testing Improvements
docs.text:
Testing coverage expanded significantly.

New focus areas include:

  • config loading
  • output directory workflows
  • JSON schema consistency
  • validation stability
  • CLI regression protection

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
Run using config:

dift --config config_sample.yaml

Generate HTML report:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --template clean

docs.title: Supported Dataset Formats
docs.text:
Supported formats remain:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
  • JSON

docs.title: Report Formats
docs.text:
Supported outputs:

  • console report
  • JSON report
  • CSV report
  • Excel report
  • HTML report

docs.title: Internal Improvements
docs.text:
Internal improvements include:

  • cleaner report architecture
  • improved config loading
  • reusable threshold handling
  • better validation organization

docs.title: Developer Experience Improvements
docs.text:
Developer workflows were improved through:

  • expanded testing coverage
  • clearer validation behavior
  • improved report consistency

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install:

pip install dift-cli

Upgrade:

pip install --upgrade dift-cli

docs.title: Looking Ahead
docs.text:
Future releases will focus on:

  • SQL database support
  • warehouse integrations
  • automation workflows
  • saved profiles
  • scheduling systems
  • historical drift tracking
  • batch comparisons

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
Current limitations:

  • no SQL connectors yet
  • no warehouse integrations yet
  • no scheduling system yet
  • no batch comparison workflows yet

These are planned for future releases.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Dift continues evolving toward becoming the open-source standard for:

  • dataset regression testing
  • data trust validation
  • ML dataset drift monitoring
  • warehouse comparison workflows
  • automated data quality enforcement

docs.title: Thank You
docs.text:
Thank you to everyone contributing feedback, ideas, testing, documentation improvements, and early feature discussions during Dift’s rapid growth.

Dift v0.3.0 represents a major step toward scalable and reusable data trust workflows.

docs.title: Dift v0.5.0 Release Notes
docs.text: Release Date: May 12, 2026

docs.title: Dift v0.5.0
docs.text:
Dift v0.5.0 is a major release focused on advanced drift detection, automation workflows, reusable validation systems, reporting improvements, and scalable comparison orchestration.

This release transforms Dift from a dataset comparison CLI into a significantly more powerful data trust and validation platform.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
Dift v0.5.0 introduces:

  • numeric drift detection
  • categorical drift analysis
  • outlier detection
  • outlier severity scoring
  • frequency distribution shift analysis
  • reusable threshold policies
  • saved comparison profiles
  • scheduled comparison workflows
  • batch dataset comparison
  • comparison history tracking
  • automation-friendly execution
  • strict exit codes
  • quiet mode
  • no-color mode
  • improved HTML reports
  • improved Excel reports
  • improved CSV summaries
  • environment-based configurations
  • reusable environment workflows
  • improved risk scoring
  • stronger CLI UX
  • expanded testing coverage

docs.title: Numeric Drift Detection
docs.text:
Dift now supports advanced numeric drift analysis.

Features include:

  • mean shift detection
  • standard deviation drift
  • range drift analysis
  • configurable drift thresholds
  • severity classification

docs.title: Numeric Drift Example
docs.text:
Numeric drift:
'revenue'
mean shift 900.00%
(high, threshold 0.1)

docs.title: Categorical Drift Detection
docs.text:
Dift now detects categorical distribution shifts.

Features include:

  • new categorical value detection
  • removed value detection
  • frequency shift analysis
  • severity classification

docs.title: Example Categorical Shift
docs.text:
Categorical shift:
'segment'
max frequency shift 60.00%
(high)

docs.title: Outlier Detection
docs.text:
Dift now includes outlier analysis using IQR-based detection.

Features include:

  • IQR outlier detection
  • outlier spike analysis
  • outlier percentage tracking
  • risk integration

docs.title: Example Outlier Warning
docs.text:
Outlier spike:
'revenue' increased by 100.00%
(high)

docs.title: Improved Risk Scoring
docs.text:
Risk scoring was expanded to include:

  • numeric drift severity
  • categorical drift severity
  • outlier spike severity
  • weighted risk calculation

This improves overall trust analysis.

docs.title: Reusable Threshold Policies
docs.text:
Threshold configurations became significantly more powerful in v0.5.0.

Supported threshold types:

  • numeric thresholds
  • categorical thresholds
  • outlier thresholds
  • column-specific overrides

docs.title: Column-Level Threshold Overrides
docs.text:
Example:

thresholds:
  columns:
    revenue:
      numeric: 0.05
      outlier: 0.1

This enables highly granular validation workflows.

docs.title: Environment-Based Configurations
docs.text:
Dift now supports reusable environment configurations.

Example:

environments:
  development:
    threshold: 0.2

  production:
    threshold: 0.05

docs.title: Environment Variable Support
docs.text:
Environment variable interpolation is now supported.

Example:

old_dataset: ${OLD_DATASET}
new_dataset: ${NEW_DATASET}

This improves:

  • CI/CD workflows
  • automation pipelines
  • deployment flexibility

docs.title: Saved Comparison Profiles
docs.text:
Dift now supports reusable saved comparison profiles.

Create profile:

dift profile create nightly-check \
  --old old.csv \
  --new new.csv \
  --key customer_id

Run profile:

dift profile run nightly-check

docs.title: Profile Benefits
docs.text:
Profiles help support:

  • recurring validations
  • nightly checks
  • reusable workflows
  • standardized comparisons

docs.title: Scheduled Comparison Workflows
docs.text:
Dift now supports automation-ready scheduling workflows.

Generate cron commands:

dift schedule cron nightly-check

Example output:

0 2 * * * dift profile run nightly-check

docs.title: Saved Schedules
docs.text:
Create reusable schedules:

dift schedule create daily-check \
  --profile nightly-check \
  --cron "0 2 * * *"

docs.title: Batch Dataset Comparison
docs.text:
Dift now supports comparing entire folders of datasets.

Example:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --key id

docs.title: Batch Workflow Features
docs.text:
Batch comparisons support:

  • folder-based matching
  • report generation
  • history tracking
  • continue-on-error workflows
  • stop-on-error workflows

docs.title: Batch HTML Reporting
docs.text:
Example:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new \
  --report html \
  --output-dir reports/batch

docs.title: Comparison History Tracking
docs.text:
Dift now supports persistent comparison history.

Enable history:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --history

docs.title: History Features
docs.text:
History workflows support:

  • historical drift tracking
  • recurring risk monitoring
  • long-term comparison visibility

docs.title: View History
docs.text:
List saved history:

dift history list

Show detailed record:

dift history show 1

docs.title: Automation-Friendly Execution
docs.text:
Dift now includes automation-focused execution behavior.

Features include:

  • strict exit codes
  • quiet mode
  • no-color mode

docs.title: Strict Exit Codes
docs.text:
Enable automation-safe risk exits:

dift prod.csv staging.csv \
  --strict-exit-codes

Exit mapping:

Exit Code Meaning 0 Low risk 1 Medium risk 2 High risk 3 Runtime failure

docs.title: Quiet Mode
docs.text:
Suppress non-error output:

dift old.csv new.csv --quiet

docs.title: No-Color Mode
docs.text:
Disable ANSI colors:

dift old.csv new.csv --no-color

Useful for:

  • CI logs
  • automation systems
  • plain-text terminals

docs.title: Improved HTML Reports
docs.text:
HTML reporting received major improvements.

Enhancements include:

  • improved layouts
  • severity badges
  • drift highlighting
  • responsive design
  • cleaner summaries

docs.title: HTML Templates
docs.text:
Dift now supports customizable HTML templates.

Example:

dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --template dark

Available templates:

  • default
  • clean
  • compact
  • enterprise
  • dark

docs.title: Improved Excel Reports
docs.text:
Excel reporting improvements include:

  • conditional formatting
  • severity highlighting
  • improved worksheet structure
  • readability improvements

docs.title: Improved CSV Reports
docs.text:
CSV reporting now includes:

  • cleaner summaries
  • drift visibility
  • improved automation compatibility

docs.title: Improved JSON Reports
docs.text:
JSON reports now provide:

  • cleaner structure
  • metadata support
  • stronger automation consistency

docs.title: Metadata Expansion
docs.text:
Report metadata now includes:

  • timestamps
  • report type
  • runtime information
  • tool version

docs.title: Improved Validation Errors
docs.text:
Validation UX was improved significantly.

Examples include:

  • clearer missing dataset guidance
  • connector installation hints
  • improved unsupported format errors
  • actionable workflow guidance

docs.title: Improved CLI UX
docs.text:
CLI usability improvements include:

  • clearer help output
  • improved examples
  • better validation messaging
  • automation-friendly workflows

docs.title: Expanded Testing Coverage
docs.text:
Testing coverage expanded significantly.

New focus areas include:

  • automation workflows
  • connector validation
  • report consistency
  • config workflows
  • batch comparisons
  • history tracking

docs.title: Internal Improvements
docs.text:
Major internal improvements include:

  • reusable threshold architecture
  • cleaner report rendering
  • modular workflow organization
  • improved validation systems

docs.title: Supported Dataset Formats
docs.text:
Supported local formats:

  • CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
  • JSON

docs.title: Report Formats
docs.text:
Supported outputs:

  • console report
  • JSON report
  • CSV report
  • Excel report
  • HTML report

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
Basic comparison:

dift old.csv new.csv --key customer_id

Drift detection:

dift old_drift.csv new_drift.csv \
  --key id \
  --threshold 0.1

Batch comparison:

dift batch \
  --old-dir data/old \
  --new-dir data/new

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install:

pip install dift-cli

Upgrade:

pip install --upgrade dift-cli

docs.title: Looking Ahead
docs.text:
Future releases will focus on:

  • SQL database support
  • warehouse integrations
  • DuckDB support
  • BigQuery support
  • plugin preparation
  • connector registry architecture
  • enterprise connector workflows

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
Current limitations:

  • no SQL database support yet
  • no warehouse connectors yet
  • no plugin architecture yet
  • no distributed execution yet

These are planned for future releases.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Dift continues evolving toward becoming the open-source standard for:

  • dataset regression testing
  • data drift monitoring
  • warehouse trust validation
  • ML dataset validation
  • automated data quality enforcement

docs.title: Thank You
docs.text:
Thank you to everyone contributing ideas, testing workflows, reporting issues, improving documentation, and helping shape the direction of Dift.

Dift v0.5.0 represents a major milestone in the platform’s evolution toward scalable enterprise-grade data trust workflows.

docs.title: Dift v0.6.0 Release Notes
docs.text: Release Date: May 20, 2026

docs.title: Dift v0.6.0
docs.text:
Dift v0.6.0 is one of the largest architectural releases so far.

This release introduces database connectors, cloud warehouse support, centralized reader architecture, connector registries, progress indicators, internal plugin preparation, and major improvements to developer extensibility.

Dift evolves in this release from a local dataset comparison tool into a scalable data trust platform capable of supporting modern analytical workflows across files, databases, and warehouses.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
Dift v0.6.0 introduces:

  • SQL database support
  • SQLite support
  • PostgreSQL support
  • MySQL support
  • Redshift support
  • Snowflake support
  • BigQuery support
  • DuckDB support
  • centralized reader registry architecture
  • modular connector interfaces
  • plugin preparation architecture
  • reusable reader abstractions
  • progress indicators
  • connector routing improvements
  • connector validation improvements
  • improved dependency guidance
  • improved CLI responsiveness
  • improved warehouse workflows
  • expanded connector testing
  • warehouse mocking support
  • improved validation UX
  • cleaner internal architecture

docs.title: SQL Database Support
docs.text:
Dift now supports direct SQL-based dataset comparison using SQLAlchemy-compatible connection strings.

Supported workflows include:

  • database-to-database comparison
  • table-to-table comparison
  • warehouse validation
  • ETL validation
  • query-driven workflows

docs.title: Supported SQL Systems
docs.text:
Supported SQL connectors include:

  • SQLite
  • PostgreSQL
  • MySQL
  • Redshift
  • Snowflake

docs.title: SQLite Support
docs.text:
Example:

dift sqlite:///examples/old.db:customers_old \
     sqlite:///examples/new.db:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: PostgreSQL Support
docs.text:
Example:

dift postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/sales_db:customers_old \
     postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/sales_db:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

Alternative psycopg driver support:

dift postgresql+psycopg://user:password@localhost:5432/sales_db:customers_old \
     postgresql+psycopg://user:password@localhost:5432/sales_db:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: MySQL Support
docs.text:
Example:

dift mysql+pymysql://user:password@localhost:3306/sales_db:customers_old \
     mysql+pymysql://user:password@localhost:3306/sales_db:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: Redshift Support
docs.text:
Example:

dift redshift+redshift_connector://user:password@cluster.region.redshift.amazonaws.com:5439/dev:orders_old \
     redshift+redshift_connector://user:password@cluster.region.redshift.amazonaws.com:5439/dev:orders_new \
     --key order_id

docs.title: Snowflake Support
docs.text:
Example:

dift snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_old \
     snowflake://user:password@account/db/schema?warehouse=compute_wh:orders_new \
     --key order_id

docs.title: BigQuery Support
docs.text:
Dift now supports direct Google BigQuery dataset comparison.

Example:

dift bigquery://my-project.analytics.customers_old \
     bigquery://my-project.analytics.customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: BigQuery Features
docs.text:
BigQuery workflows support:

  • warehouse comparisons
  • cloud analytical validation
  • service account authentication
  • query-driven workflows

docs.title: BigQuery Authentication
docs.text:
Dift uses standard Google Cloud authentication workflows.

Example:

export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/service-account.json"

docs.title: DuckDB Support
docs.text:
Dift now supports analytical workflows using DuckDB databases.

Example:

dift duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb:customers_old \
     duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: DuckDB Workflow Benefits
docs.text:
DuckDB support enables:

  • local analytical warehouses
  • Parquet interoperability
  • SQL-driven comparisons
  • lightweight warehouse validation

docs.title: Centralized Reader Registry Architecture
docs.text:
v0.6.0 introduces a major internal architectural improvement:

Reader Registry System

This centralizes dataset routing and connector discovery.

docs.title: Reader Registry Benefits
docs.text:
The new architecture enables:

  • centralized connector routing
  • modular connector design
  • reusable validation workflows
  • future plugin preparation
  • dynamic reader registration

docs.title: Shared Reader Interface
docs.text:
Dift now uses standardized reader interfaces.

Example:

class BaseReader:
    def can_handle(self, source: str) -> bool:
        ...

    def read(self, source: str):
        ...

docs.title: Modular Reader Architecture
docs.text:
The new architecture separates connectors into dedicated readers.

Examples include:

  • LocalFileReader
  • SQLReader
  • DuckDBReader
  • BigQueryReader

docs.title: Connector Registry System
docs.text:
Dift now includes centralized connector registration.

Example:

registry.register(SQLReader())
registry.register(BigQueryReader())

docs.title: Centralized Routing
docs.text:
Dataset loading now follows:

CLI
  ↓
Registry
  ↓
Reader
  ↓
Polars DataFrame

This significantly reduces connector coupling.

docs.title: Plugin Preparation Architecture
docs.text:
v0.6.0 lays the internal foundation for future plugin ecosystems.

Future goals include:

  • third-party connectors
  • enterprise extensions
  • optional integrations
  • community-maintained plugins

docs.title: Future Plugin Possibilities
docs.text:
Potential future structure:

dift/plugins/
├── snowflake/
├── databricks/
├── kafka/
├── s3/
└── spark/

docs.title: Connector Isolation Improvements
docs.text:
Connectors are now significantly more isolated from the comparison engine.

Benefits include:

  • cleaner maintenance
  • scalable architecture
  • easier testing
  • optional dependency preparation

docs.title: Progress Indicators
docs.text:
Dift now includes lightweight progress indicators for long-running operations.

Progress coverage includes:

  • dataset loading
  • SQL loading
  • warehouse queries
  • comparison execution
  • report generation

docs.title: Progress Indicator Goals
docs.text:
Progress indicators improve:

  • CLI responsiveness
  • warehouse UX
  • large dataset workflows
  • automation visibility

docs.title: Improved Validation UX
docs.text:
Validation workflows were significantly improved.

Enhancements include:

  • clearer connector guidance
  • better dependency installation help
  • improved unsupported URI handling
  • actionable validation messages

docs.title: Example Validation Error
docs.text:
PostgreSQL support requires psycopg2.

Install it with:
  pip install psycopg2-binary

docs.title: Improved Unsupported Format Errors
docs.text:
Example:

Unsupported dataset type '.txt'.

Supported local file types:
.csv, .json, .parquet, .xlsx

docs.title: Expanded Connector Testing
docs.text:
Testing coverage expanded heavily in v0.6.0.

New testing areas include:

  • SQL connector testing
  • warehouse mocking
  • connector routing
  • registry behavior
  • URI parsing
  • dependency guidance
  • progress indicator workflows

docs.title: Warehouse Mock Testing
docs.text:
Warehouse integrations now support mocked testing workflows for:

  • BigQuery
  • Snowflake
  • Redshift

This improves:

  • offline development
  • reproducibility
  • CI stability

docs.title: Internal Refactoring
docs.text:
Major internal refactors include:

  • reusable connector validation
  • centralized routing logic
  • modular dataset readers
  • cleaner error handling
  • reusable connector abstractions

docs.title: Improved Developer Extensibility
docs.text:
The new architecture significantly improves future extensibility.

New connectors can now be added with far fewer core modifications.

docs.title: Optional Dependency Preparation
docs.text:
Connector dependencies are now more isolated.

Examples:

pip install sqlalchemy
pip install duckdb
pip install google-cloud-bigquery

This reduces unnecessary installation overhead.

docs.title: Improved CLI UX
docs.text:
CLI workflows now provide:

  • clearer connector guidance
  • improved progress visibility
  • stronger validation messaging
  • better warehouse workflows

docs.title: Supported Dataset Sources
docs.text: Dift v0.6.0 supports:

docs.title: Local Files
docs.text:
• CSV
  • Parquet
  • Excel
  • JSON

docs.title: Databases & Warehouses
docs.text:
• SQLite
  • PostgreSQL
  • MySQL
  • DuckDB
  • BigQuery
  • Redshift
  • Snowflake

docs.title: Supported Report Formats
docs.text:
Supported outputs:

  • console report
  • JSON report
  • CSV report
  • Excel report
  • HTML report

docs.title: Compare PostgreSQL Tables
docs.text:
dift postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/db:customers_old \
     postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/db:customers_new \
     --key customer_id

docs.title: Compare DuckDB Tables
docs.text:
dift duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb:orders_old \
     duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb:orders_new \
     --key order_id

docs.title: Compare BigQuery Tables
docs.text:
dift bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_old \
     bigquery://analytics.sales.orders_new \
     --key order_id

docs.title: Generate HTML Report
docs.text:
dift old.csv new.csv \
  --report html \
  --template enterprise \
  --output report.html

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install Dift:

pip install dift-cli

Upgrade:

pip install --upgrade dift-cli

docs.title: Optional Connector Dependencies
docs.text:
Install SQL support:

pip install sqlalchemy

Install PostgreSQL support:

pip install psycopg2-binary

Install MySQL support:

pip install pymysql

Install BigQuery support:

pip install google-cloud-bigquery db-dtypes

Install DuckDB support:

pip install duckdb

docs.title: Architecture Milestone
docs.text:
v0.6.0 represents a major architectural milestone for Dift.

The platform now includes:

  • scalable connector routing
  • reusable reader abstractions
  • plugin preparation
  • warehouse-ready workflows
  • extensible connector architecture

This release lays the groundwork for future ecosystem expansion.

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
Current limitations:

  • no external plugin loading yet
  • no distributed execution yet
  • no streaming connectors yet
  • no async connector execution yet

These are planned for future releases.

docs.title: Looking Ahead
docs.text:
Future releases may focus on:

  • plugin ecosystems
  • Databricks support
  • S3 support
  • Spark support
  • Kafka support
  • distributed execution
  • streaming validation
  • enterprise workflows

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Dift continues evolving toward becoming the open-source standard for:

  • dataset regression testing
  • warehouse trust validation
  • data drift monitoring
  • ML data validation
  • automated data quality enforcement
  • enterprise data trust workflows

docs.title: Thank You
docs.text:
Thank you to everyone contributing ideas, feedback, testing, architecture discussions, validation improvements, and connector workflows throughout Dift’s rapid evolution.

Dift v0.6.0 marks the beginning of Dift’s transition into a scalable connector-driven data trust platform.
